title: What is ACS AEM Commons & Deploying It
text:
**ACS AEM Commons** is a widely-used, open-source library of ready-made tools, services and components for AEM — maintained by the Adobe community (Adobe Consulting Services). Instead of re-inventing common plumbing, you install it and configure the bits you need. ## What's in the box (themes) ```mermaid flowchart TD ACS["ACS AEM Commons"] --> Ops["Ops / Dispatcher
flush rules, health checks"] ACS --> Authoring["Authoring
shared component props, page redirects"] ACS --> Bulk["Bulk content
MCP, Fast Action Manager"] ACS --> Util["Utilities
named transforms, error pages, JCR tools"] ``` ## Deploying it ACS Commons is a content package + bundle. The clean way is to add it to your project's **`all`** package as a dependency so it deploys with everything else: ```xml com.adobe.acs acs-aem-commons-all ``` Embed it in the `all` package's `filevault-package` config, or — for a quick local try — upload the released package via **Package Manager** (`/crx/packmgr`). After install, its features live under **Tools → ACS AEM Commons** and as configurable OSGi services. Pick the ACS Commons version that matches your **AEM version** (and AEMaaCS vs 6.5 — there are different artifacts). A mismatch can fail to activate bundles. Treat ACS Commons like any dependency: pin a version, embed it in `all`, and deploy it through your pipeline — not by hand on each environment. Then enable only the features you actually use.

title: Useful ACS Commons Features
text:
A tour of the features you'll reach for most. Each is opt-in — enable via an OSGi config or a tool UI.

## Operations & dispatcher

- **Dispatcher Flush Rules** — when page A is activated, also flush related paths B/C from the dispatcher cache (e.g. flush the homepage when any child changes). Solves stale-cache problems beyond the default tree flush.
- **Health Checks** — ready-made Sling Health Checks (and a dashboard) for monitoring.
- **Error Page Handler** — map error codes to friendly, authorable error pages per site.

## Authoring

- **Shared / Global Component Properties** — values authored once at the page or site level and read by many component instances (e.g. a shared phone number) — no repeating fields per component.
- **Redirect Manager** — author and manage vanity/SEO redirects in a UI, backed by a redirect map.
- **Pages Reference Provider / Component-level tools** — surface where content is reused.

## Bulk content & data

- **MCP (Manage Controlled Processes)** — a framework + UI for safe, resumable bulk operations: bulk asset import/ingestion, tag/property updates, folder restructuring, with reporting.
- **Fast Action Manager** — high-throughput engine for processing large content sets efficiently.

## Utilities

- **Named Image Transforms** — define a named server-side image transform (crop/resize/greyscale) and request it via a suffix — handy for on-the-fly variants.
- **JCR / Query tools, Workflow utilities, On-Deploy Scripts** — assorted developer helpers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Activate["Activate page A"] --> Rule["ACS Dispatcher Flush Rule"]
  Rule --> FlushA["Flush /content/A"]
  Rule --> FlushHome["Flush /content/home (related)"]
```

<Callout type="do">Don't hand-roll dispatcher flush logic, bulk-update scripts, or redirect handling — ACS Commons has battle-tested versions. Enable the specific feature's OSGi config, and read its docs for the exact properties.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">ACS Commons is community-maintained, not Adobe-supported product code. It's excellent and ubiquitous — but vet each feature for your AEM version and pin the dependency, as with any third-party library.</Callout>

title: Assignment: Put ACS Commons to Work
text:
Install ACS Commons and use two features end to end.

## 1 — Deploy

- [ ] Add ACS Commons (matching your AEM version) and deploy it; confirm **Tools → ACS AEM Commons** appears

## 2 — A dispatcher flush rule

- [ ] Configure a **Dispatcher Flush Rule**: activating any child under `/content/academy` also flushes the homepage
- [ ] Activate a child page and confirm the homepage is invalidated in the dispatcher cache

## 3 — Shared component properties

- [ ] Enable **Shared/Global Component Properties**; author a shared value (e.g. a contact phone) at page level
- [ ] Read it from two different component instances

## 4 — Explore MCP

- [ ] Open **MCP** and run (or inspect) a controlled process — e.g. a bulk property update on a test folder — and read its report

<Callout type="do">Done when ACS Commons is deployed through your build, a flush rule keeps the homepage fresh when children change, two components share one authored value, and you've seen MCP run a safe bulk operation with reporting.</Callout>

title: Replication & Activation
text:
AEM is split into **author** (where you create content) and **publish** (what visitors see). Getting content from one to the other is **replication** — and pressing *Publish* / *Activate* is what triggers it. 📊 Companion slides for this module: [AEM Advanced deck](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1PG2VZns1v2ynAvEOrh66APhP20RlAafEXWyf8l-HS18/edit?usp=sharing). ## The author → publish model ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Author (:4502)
create + activate"] -->|"replication agent"| P["Publish (:4503)
serves content"] P --> V["Visitors"] ``` - **Author** — the authoring environment; not public-facing. - **Publish** — serves the live site; receives content via replication. - **Replication agent** — the configured "pipe" that pushes activated content to a publish instance. ## The default replication agent Locally, author talks to publish through the **Default Agent**. Configure it once: 1. Open **Tools → Deployment → Replication → Agents on author** (`/etc/replication`) and open the **Default Agent**. 2. **Edit → Transport** tab, and point the **URI** at the publish *receive* servlet: ```text http://localhost:4503/bin/receive?sling:authRequestLogin=1 ``` 3. Set the transport **user / password** (locally `admin` / `admin`). 4. **Save**, then **Test Connection** — you should get a successful handshake. The `receive` servlet on publish is the endpoint that *accepts* replicated content. `sling:authRequestLogin=1` forces an auth challenge so the agent's credentials are actually used. ## Activate a page With the agent working, publish content: 1. Select a page (e.g. `/content/academy/language-master/en`) and **Publish** it. 2. Verify it on publish: `http://localhost:4503/content/academy/language-master/en.html`. Activation failing? Check the agent's **Test Connection** first, then confirm the publish instance is running and reachable at the Transport URI. **Reverse replication** (publish → author) brings user-generated content — form posts, comments — back to author through a *reverse* agent. Same mechanism, opposite direction.

title: DAM: Assets, Metadata & Renditions
text:
The **Digital Asset Management** (DAM) console — `/assets.html/content/dam` — stores images, video and
documents, plus the **metadata** AEM extracts from them.

## Uploading & metadata

Upload an image to the DAM and open it: AEM has automatically extracted its **metadata**, including
**camera data** (EXIF — camera model, exposure, GPS, …) when the original photo carries it.

<Callout type="note">Two images can look alike but carry very different metadata: a shot straight from a camera keeps full EXIF, while one exported or processed may have it stripped. Open both and compare the *Camera Data* to see the difference.</Callout>

## Renditions — why the published image is smaller

Add a DAM image to a page with the **Image** component, publish, and the file visitors download is
usually **smaller** than the original you uploaded. AEM serves an optimised **rendition**, not the raw
asset:

- On ingestion, DAM workflows generate **renditions** — resized, re-encoded copies — alongside the original.
- The Core Image component requests an appropriately sized, web-optimised rendition (often WebP) for the viewport.

Try it: add an asset to `/content/academy/language-master/en`, publish, then **Save image as** from the
published page and compare its size to the original — the delta is the rendition doing its job.

<Callout type="do">Upload the **highest-quality original** and let AEM generate the optimised renditions. Never hand-shrink images before upload — you throw away quality the renditions could have used.</Callout>

title: Workflows: Asset (De)activation
text:
A **workflow** automates a multi-step process — reviews, publishing, asset processing — combining
**human** and **automatic** steps.

## Workflow models

Open the **Workflow Models** console (`/libs/cq/workflow/admin/console/content/models.html`). A model
is a sequence between **Flow Start** and **Flow End**. The two step types you'll use most:

- **Participant step** — a *human* task: it assigns work to a user or group and waits in their **Inbox**.
- **Process step** — an *automatic* task: it runs a server-side process (e.g. activate / deactivate).

## Build an asset-deactivation workflow

1. **Create** a model, e.g. *"NC: Asset deactivation"*.
2. Remove every step except **Flow Start** and **Flow End**.
3. Add a **Participant step** titled *"Deactivate asset"*, assigned to a user or group (the user you make in *Users & Permissions* works well) — someone must approve before it proceeds.
4. Add a **process step** that **deactivates** the page/asset (takes it down from publish).
5. **Save**, then run the model against an asset that's currently live on publish.

The assignee approves the task in their Inbox; the process step then removes the asset from publish.

<Callout type="note">Workflows run manually (from the console or Inbox) or automatically via **workflow launchers** that watch a path + event — e.g. "on asset upload, run metadata processing."</Callout>

title: Users, Groups & Permissions
text:
AEM access is managed in the **Security** console — **Tools → Security** (`/security/users.html`).

## Users vs groups

- A **user** is an individual account.
- A **group** carries a set of **permissions**; you grant access by adding users to groups — **not** by
  assigning permissions to each user one by one. Groups keep access manageable as people join and leave.

## Create a user

1. Security → **Users → Create**.
2. Give it an **ID** (e.g. `tiger-woods`) and a password.
3. Under **Groups**, add it to a group — e.g. `administrators` for full access.
4. **Save**.

Test it: open an **incognito** window and log in as the new user — you'll see exactly what that
group's permissions allow.

<Callout type="do">Prefer **groups** over per-user permissions, and grant the **least privilege** that does the job. An `administrators` member can do anything — hand it out sparingly.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">Clicking through the Security UI doesn't scale and doesn't survive across environments. The next lesson — the **AC Tool** — manages users, groups, and ACLs **as code**, applied identically on every deploy.</Callout>

title: MSM: Live Copies & Rollout
text:
**Multi-Site Management** (MSM) lets one source site drive many derived sites — translations, regional variants — without copy-pasting content. ## The vocabulary - **Blueprint / language master** — the source site (e.g. `/content/academy/language-master/en`). - **Live copy** — a derived site that **inherits** from the master. - **Rollout config** — the rules deciding *what* syncs from master to live copy and *when*. - **Rollout** — the act of pushing master changes down into the live copies. ```mermaid flowchart TD BP["Blueprint / Language Master
/content/academy/language-master/en"] -->|"rollout config"| LC1["Live Copy: au"] BP -->|"rollout"| LC2["Live Copy: de"] LC1 -->|"break inheritance"| Local["Locally edited component
(rollout skips it)"] ``` ## Create a live copy 1. In **Sites**, select the source — the English **language master**. 2. **Create → Live Copy**. 3. Choose a **destination** (e.g. under *Academy*), a **title** ("Australian homepage") and a **name** (`au`). 4. Pick a **Rollout config** — *Standard rollout config* — and **Save**. You now have `…/academy/au`, mirroring the master. ## Inheritance Each component in a live copy **inherits** from the master by default. Edit one locally and you **break inheritance** for that component (a chain icon marks it as detached) — a later rollout won't overwrite your local change. Re-enable inheritance to resume syncing. Author shared content **once** in the master and roll it out. Only break inheritance where a region genuinely differs — every break is something you then maintain by hand.

title: AC Tool: ACLs as Code
text:
The **[Access Control Tool](https://github.com/Netcentric/accesscontroltool)** (AC Tool) is a
Netcentric open-source tool that manages AEM **users, groups, and ACLs declaratively** — you describe
permissions in **YAML**, and the tool applies them repeatably on every deploy, instead of clicking
through the Security console per environment.

## Install (manual, for this exercise)

1. Download the latest package from **Maven Central**.
2. **Package Manager** on **author** → upload + install the `.zip`. Repeat on **publish**.
3. Configuration console → **Main → AC Tool → OSGi config** → set the path where your ACL YAML lives
   (e.g. `/apps/academy/acls`), and save. Repeat on publish.

<Callout type="note">On real projects you don't do this by hand — Netcentric's Maven plugins deploy the AC Tool and apply the ACLs automatically as part of the build.</Callout>

## How the config works

Drop **YAML files** in your configured folder. Each file has sections defining **groups**, **users**,
and **ACEs** (access-control entries). **Order matters** — a group must be defined before it's
referenced — so prefix filenames with numbers to control read order.

```yaml
# 10-groups.yaml  (illustrative)
group_config:
  fragment-authors:
    - path: /content/academy
      permission: allow
      actions: read
```

After deploying the YAML, open the **AC Tool console** in the System Console and click **Apply** —
then re-check the permissions; they now reflect the YAML.

## Two examples

- **Anonymous access on publish** — a `fragment-everyone` group grants the `anonymous` user read
  access so the public site is viewable. Install on publish, **Apply**, then re-check anonymous's permissions.
- **A privileged user on author** — an `everyone` group tightens the defaults, a `fragment-authors`
  group grants read to `/content/academy`, and an `Academy` user is added to it. Install on author,
  **Apply**, then log in (incognito) as `Academy`.

## Fragments & best practice

- A **fragment** is a reusable bundle of ACLs; attach several fragments to a group to compose its access.
- Prefer **groups + fragments** over per-user grants.
- Adding real users to **author** isn't recommended (test users only) — and you can scope those to
  **dev only**, never production, using AEM **run modes** in the AC Tool config.

<Callout type="do">Permissions as code = the same reviewable access on every environment. See the [AC Tool configuration docs](https://github.com/Netcentric/accesscontroltool/blob/develop/docs/Configuration.md) for the full YAML reference.</Callout>

title: The Dispatcher: Caching & Security
text:
In production, visitors don't hit publish directly — they go through the **Dispatcher**, an Apache HTTP Server module in front of publish that is both the **caching layer** and the **security layer**. ```mermaid flowchart LR V["Visitor"] --> D["Dispatcher
(Apache module)"] D -->|"cache hit"| Cache[("Cached file on disk")] D -->|"miss + allowed"| P["Publish (:4503)"] P --> D D -.->|"blocked by /filter"| X["403 / 404"] ``` ## What it does - **Caches** rendered pages and assets to disk and serves them, so most requests never reach AEM — big for performance and resilience. - **Filters** requests (security): a deny-by-default ruleset that only lets safe paths/extensions/methods through. - **Load-balances** across multiple publish instances. ## Caching & invalidation The dispatcher caches a `GET` response the first time it's requested, then serves that file until it's **invalidated**: - Configured in `dispatcher.any` under a farm's `/cache` (`/rules`, `/invalidate`, `/statfileslevel`). - **Not cached:** authenticated requests, most query-string URLs, non-`GET` methods. - **Invalidation:** when a page is **activated**, publish's **Dispatcher Flush** replication agent sends an invalidation request; the dispatcher drops the affected cache (often by touching a **statfile** that ages out a whole tree). This ties back to *Replication*: activate → publish → the flush agent pings the dispatcher → stale cache is invalidated, so visitors get the fresh page. ## Security filters The `/filter` section is **deny by default** — you explicitly allow what's public: ```text # deny everything, then allow specific, safe requests /0001 { /type "deny" /glob "*" } /0010 { /type "allow" /url "/content/*" } # only the content tree ``` Block the dangerous bits: `.json` / `.infinity` selectors, `/bin/*` and `/system/*`, cache-busting query selectors, and non-`GET` methods on public URLs. ## Practice - [ ] Inspect a `dispatcher.any`: find the `/cache /rules` (what's cacheable) and the `/filter` (what's allowed). - [ ] Activate a page and confirm the cached file appears in the dispatcher docroot; re-activate and confirm it's invalidated. - [ ] Add a deny rule for `*.json` under `/content` and verify that path is now blocked through the dispatcher. A page works on publish (`:4503`) but not through the dispatcher? It's almost always a **filter** blocking it or a stale **cache** — check those two first.

title: OSGi Configuration & Run Modes
text:
AEM runs on an **OSGi** container: every service — servlets, schedulers, replication agents, your own
components — is tuned through **OSGi configurations**.

## The OSGi console

The Web Console at `/system/console/configMgr` lists every configurable service. Edit a config there
and it takes effect immediately — but a console edit is **not** version-controlled and is lost on a
fresh environment. Use it to *inspect* and *experiment*, not as the source of truth.

## Persisting configs in the repository

For real projects, configs live in the codebase under your app as **`.cfg.json`** files (or
`sling:OsgiConfig` nodes):

```text
ui.apps/.../apps/academy/osgiconfig/config/com.example.MyService.cfg.json
```

They deploy with the package, so every environment gets the same config — and it's reviewable in git.

## Run modes — per-environment config

A **run mode** is a label AEM starts with. `author` / `publish` are set automatically; you add custom
ones like `dev` / `stage` / `prod`. AEM applies the config folder(s) whose run modes all match:

```text
config/                  ← always applied
config.author/           ← only on author
config.publish/          ← only on publish
config.publish.prod/     ← only when BOTH publish AND prod are active
```

Run modes are decided at startup (the quickstart jar filename, or `-Dsling.run.modes=publish,prod`).

<Callout type="do">Put environment-specific values (service URLs, credentials, feature flags) in **run-mode config folders**, not in code. Same build artifact, different config per environment.</Callout>

## Practice

- [ ] In `/system/console/configMgr`, find a service (e.g. the **Day CQ Link Externalizer**) and read its current config.
- [ ] Persist a config as a `.cfg.json` under `config.publish/`, deploy, and confirm it applies **only** on publish (absent on author).
- [ ] Check the active run modes at `/system/console/status-slingsettings`.

title: Content Packages & Deployment
text:
A **content package** is how JCR content and code move between AEM instances — a zip of repository
content plus a **filter** that says exactly what's inside.

## Anatomy (FileVault)

```text
my-package.zip
├── jcr_root/…                    ← the actual content/code (e.g. /apps/academy, /content/academy)
└── META-INF/vault/filter.xml     ← which repository paths are in the package
```

`filter.xml` defines **filter roots** and an import **mode**:

```xml
<filter root="/apps/academy"/>
<filter root="/content/academy" mode="merge"/>
```

- **replace** (default) — the package *owns* that subtree; install overwrites everything under it.
- **merge** / **update** — only add/update nodes; don't delete what's not in the package.

## Package Manager

At `/crx/packmgr/index.jsp` you can **build**, **download**, **upload**, **install**, and **replicate**
packages by hand — handy for moving content between environments or snapshotting a subtree.

## In a project

Maven's **filevault-package** plugin builds the `ui.apps` / `ui.content` packages, and the **`all`**
package embeds them for a single install. You've used these profiles:

```bash
mvn clean install -PautoInstallSinglePackage          # builds + installs the all package
mvn clean install -PautoInstallPackage -pl ui.apps    # installs one module's package
```

<Callout type="warn">A too-broad filter root in **replace** mode can wipe content on install (it owns the whole subtree). Scope filters tightly, and use **merge/update** for shared trees like `/content`.</Callout>

## Practice

- [ ] In Package Manager, create a package with a filter on `/content/academy`, **build** it, download the zip, and inspect its `filter.xml`.
- [ ] Install it on another instance (or rebuild) and confirm the content arrived.
- [ ] From the project, deploy with `mvn clean install -PautoInstallPackage -pl ui.apps` and find the installed package in Package Manager.

title: Querying & Indexing (Oak)
text:
AEM's repository is **Apache Oak**. Unlike a SQL database, Oak only queries efficiently against an
**index** — an unindexed query **traverses** the content tree, which is slow and warned about in the
logs.

## Querying

- **Query Builder** — AEM's high-level predicate syntax; debug it at `/libs/cq/search/content/querydebug.html`.
- **JCR-SQL2 / XPath** — the low-level query languages Query Builder compiles down to.

```text
# Query Builder: pages under /content/academy built on a given template
path=/content/academy
type=cq:Page
property=jcr:content/cq:template
property.value=/conf/academy/settings/wcm/templates/academy-homepage
```

## Indexes

Index definitions live under **`/oak:index`**:

- **Property index** — fast exact-match lookups on a property (e.g. `cq:template`).
- **Lucene index** — full-text and complex queries; most AEM queries rely on a Lucene index.

If a query traverses, either add/extend an index or constrain the query (tighter `path`, indexed
properties).

## Spotting slow queries

- **Explain Query** shows which index a query uses — or that it traverses.
- The **Query Performance** tool (Tools → Operations → Diagnosis) lists slow/traversing queries.
- The `error.log` warns: *"Traversed N nodes … consider creating an index."*

<Callout type="do">Constrain by an **indexed** path/property and avoid `//*` over large trees. Add a custom Oak index only when no existing index can serve the query.</Callout>

## Practice

- [ ] Run a Query Builder query in the debugger; open **Explain Query** and note which index it uses.
- [ ] Write a query that **traverses** (filter on an unindexed property) and find the warning in `error.log`.
- [ ] Inspect `/oak:index` for a Lucene index (e.g. `cqPageLucene`) and read which properties it indexes.

title: Self-check
text:
Quick questions to test yourself on the module. Try to answer before revealing.

## Replication

**Where does the Default Agent's Transport URI point, and why the `sling:authRequestLogin=1` parameter?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

At the **publish** instance's `receive` servlet (`http://localhost:4503/bin/receive`). The
`sling:authRequestLogin=1` forces an authentication challenge, so the agent's configured transport
**user/password** are actually used to push the content.

</Reveal>

**After activating a page on author, how do you confirm it worked?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

Open the same path on the **publish** instance (`:4503`) — e.g. `…/content/academy/language-master/en.html`. If the agent's **Test Connection** passes and the page appears on publish, replication is working.

</Reveal>

## DAM

**You upload a 4 MB photo, add it to a page, and the published image is far smaller. Why?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

AEM serves an optimised **rendition**, not the raw asset. DAM workflows generate resized/re-encoded
copies on ingest, and the Core Image component requests an appropriately sized (often WebP) rendition
for the viewport. Always upload the **best-quality original** and let AEM downscale.

</Reveal>

## Workflows

**What's the difference between a participant step and a process step?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

A **participant step** is a *human* task — it assigns work to a user/group and waits in their Inbox.
A **process step** is *automatic* — it runs a server-side process (e.g. activate/deactivate) with no
human involvement.

</Reveal>

## Users & permissions

**Why grant access through groups rather than to individual users?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

Groups carry the permissions; you add users to groups. It keeps access **manageable and consistent**
as people join and leave, and it scales — you change a group once instead of editing every user. Aim
for **least privilege**.

</Reveal>

## MSM

**What is a live copy, and what happens when you "break inheritance" on a component?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

A **live copy** is a site derived from a master (blueprint/language master) that **inherits** its
content. Breaking inheritance on a component detaches it from the master, so a future **rollout won't
overwrite** your local change. Re-enable inheritance to resume syncing.

</Reveal>

## AC Tool

**How does the AC Tool define permissions, and why does the YAML file order matter?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

Permissions are described declaratively in **YAML** (groups, users, ACEs) and **applied** by the tool
— ideally on every deploy. Order matters because a group must be **defined before it's referenced**,
so files are read in order; prefix filenames with numbers to control it.

</Reveal>

## Dispatcher

**A page renders on publish (`:4503`) but 404s through the dispatcher. What are the two usual causes?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

Either a **security filter** is denying the request (deny-by-default; the path/extension isn't
allowed), or the **cache** is serving a stale copy that wasn't invalidated. Check the `/filter` and
the cache (statfile / flush agent) first.

</Reveal>

## OSGi & run modes

**You need a different service URL on publish-prod than on author. Where do you put it?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

In a **run-mode config folder** — e.g. a `.cfg.json` under `config.publish.prod/`. AEM applies only
the config folders whose run modes all match at startup, so the same build artifact carries different
config per environment. Don't hard-code it.

</Reveal>

## Packages

**What does the `filter.xml` mode `replace` do that `merge` doesn't — and why is that dangerous?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

`replace` makes the package **own** the filter-root subtree: install overwrites everything under it,
**deleting** nodes not in the package. `merge`/`update` only add/update. A too-broad `replace` filter
(e.g. on `/content`) can wipe live content.

</Reveal>

## Querying

**Your query is slow and the log says "Traversed N nodes." What happened and how do you fix it?**

<Reveal title="Answer">

The query had **no usable index**, so Oak walked the tree (traversal). Fix it by constraining to an
**indexed** path/property, or by adding/extending an Oak index (property or Lucene) under `/oak:index`.
Use **Explain Query** to confirm which index it uses.

</Reveal>

<Callout type="do">Comfortable with all of these? You can take content author→publish, manage assets and workflows, derive sites with MSM, manage permissions as code, run the dispatcher as a cache + security layer, configure per environment, ship content as packages, and keep queries indexed. That's the advanced AEM toolkit.</Callout>

title: Assignment: Advanced AEM Hands-on
text:
Work through these on a local **author (:4502)** + **publish (:4503)** setup with the academy content.

## 1 — Replication

- [ ] Configure the **Default Agent** transport to the publish *receive* servlet and **Test Connection**
- [ ] Activate `/content/academy/language-master/en` and confirm it on publish

## 2 — Users

- [ ] In **Security**, create a user (e.g. `tiger-woods`) in the `administrators` group; log in as them in an incognito window

## 3 — DAM

- [ ] Upload two images and compare their **camera-data** metadata
- [ ] Add one to a page, publish, then compare the **published rendition's size** to the original — and explain the difference

## 4 — Workflow

- [ ] Build an *"Asset deactivation"* workflow (participant step → deactivate step) and run it against an activated asset

## 5 — MSM

- [ ] Create a **live copy** of the English master ("Australian homepage", `au`) using the standard rollout config

## 6 — AC Tool

- [ ] Install the AC Tool on author + publish and point it at `/apps/academy/acls`
- [ ] Apply the **anonymous-access-on-publish** example; verify anonymous's permissions changed
- [ ] Apply the **privileged-user-on-author** example; log in as `Academy`

## 7 — Dispatcher

- [ ] In a `dispatcher.any`, locate the `/cache /rules` and the `/filter` section
- [ ] Add a **deny** rule for `*.json` under `/content` and confirm that path is blocked through the dispatcher
- [ ] Activate a page and confirm the cache is invalidated (the file is dropped from the dispatcher docroot)

## 8 — OSGi & run modes

- [ ] Persist an OSGi config as a `.cfg.json` under `config.publish/` so it applies **only** on publish; deploy and verify it's absent on author
- [ ] Read the active run modes at `/system/console/status-slingsettings`

## 9 — Packages

- [ ] In Package Manager, build a package filtered to `/content/academy`, download it, and inspect its `filter.xml`
- [ ] Reinstall it and confirm the content is restored

## 10 — Querying

- [ ] Run a Query Builder query in the debugger and use **Explain Query** to see which index serves it
- [ ] Force a **traversing** query and find the warning in `error.log`

<Callout type="do">Done when content flows author→publish, your workflow takes an asset down, the live copy mirrors the master, the AC Tool applies permissions straight from YAML, the dispatcher caches and blocks correctly, your config is scoped by run mode, a package round-trips your content, and you can tell an indexed query from a traversing one.</Callout>

title: Why Unit-test AEM Code
text:
A unit test runs a small piece of your Java in **isolation**, fast, with no running AEM. It documents
intent, catches regressions on every build, and lets you refactor without fear.

## What's worth testing in AEM

- **Sling Models** — given content, do the getters return the right shaped data? (highest value, easiest)
- **OSGi services** — does the business logic behave across inputs and config?
- **Servlets** — given a request, is the response correct?
- **Workflow processes / job consumers** — does the step do the right thing?

Skip testing the framework itself (HTL, the JCR) — test **your** logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  E2E["End-to-end / IT — few, slow"] --> Int["Integration (AemContext) — some"]
  Int --> Unit["Unit (JUnit + Mockito) — many, fast"]
```

Most of your tests should be fast units at the base; reach for `AemContext` only when you genuinely
touch Sling APIs.

## The toolbox

| Tool | Role |
|---|---|
| **JUnit 5** | the test runner + assertions (`@Test`, `assertEquals`) |
| **Mockito** | fake collaborators (`mock`, `when`, `verify`) |
| **AEM Mocks** (`io.wcm`) | an in-memory AEM context — resources, `ValueMap`, models, OSGi |

These live as **test-scoped** dependencies in the `core` module and run with `mvn test` — part of every
build, so a broken contract fails CI before it ships.

## The shape of a test: Arrange · Act · Assert

```java
@Test
void formatsPriceInEuros() {
  PriceFormatter f = new PriceFormatterImpl();          // arrange
  String out = f.format(1999, Locale.GERMANY);          // act
  assertEquals("19,99 €", out);                          // assert
}
```

<Callout type="do">Aim for **fast, isolated, repeatable** tests. If a test needs a real server, a network call, or sleeps, it's not a unit test — mock the boundary and test the logic.</Callout>

title: JUnit & Mockito
AEM Mocks (AemContext)
Testing Best Practices
text:
Good tests make change cheap. Bad tests make change painful. A few habits decide which you get.

## Habits

- **Test behaviour, not implementation.** Assert *what* the method returns/does, not which private calls it makes. Implementation-coupled tests break on every refactor.
- **Name the scenario.** `uppercasesTitle()`, `returnsEmptyListWhenNoChildren()` — the name should read like a spec.
- **Arrange · Act · Assert**, one logical assertion per test. Several tiny tests beat one mega-test.
- **Isolated & repeatable.** No shared mutable state between tests, no order dependence, no real clock/network. `@BeforeEach` for fresh fixtures.
- **Cover the edges.** Null, empty, missing property, the failure path — that's where bugs live, not the happy path.

## Coverage is a guide, not a goal

A high coverage number with no assertions proves nothing. Chase **meaningful** coverage: the branches
that matter (null handling, error paths, config variations). 100% of getters/setters isn't the point.

## What not to test

- The framework (HTL rendering, JCR persistence, Mockito itself).
- Trivial getters/setters with no logic.
- Third-party libraries — assume they work; test *your* use of them.

<Callout type="do">If a class is hard to test, that's a design smell — usually too many responsibilities or hidden dependencies. Split it, inject its collaborators, and the test gets easy. **Testability is a design feature.**</Callout>

title: Assignment: Test a Model & a Service
text:
Add tests to the `core` module for one Sling Model and one service.

## 1 — Service with Mockito

- [ ] Pick (or write) a service with a dependency; test the happy path with `when(...).thenReturn(...)`
- [ ] `verify()` the collaborator is called as expected
- [ ] Add a failure-path test with `thenThrow(...)`

## 2 — Sling Model with AemContext

- [ ] Load a small JSON fixture and `addModelsForClasses(...)`
- [ ] Adapt the resource to the model and assert the getters
- [ ] Register a mock `@OSGiService` dependency so injection resolves

## 3 — Edge cases

- [ ] Test the model with a **missing** property — assert the `@Default` / null-safe behaviour
- [ ] Test an **empty** child list returns an empty list, not null

## 4 — Run it

- [ ] `mvn test -pl core` is green; check the surefire report

<Callout type="do">Done when each test names a behaviour, the model survives missing/empty content, the service's happy and failure paths are both pinned, and `mvn test` passes from a clean checkout.</Callout>

title: Component Structure
text:
An AEM component is just a **folder of files under `/apps`**. At its simplest it needs two things: a node that declares "I am a component", and an HTL script that renders it. ## Where components live In this project they sit under `/apps/academy/components//`: ```text ui.apps/src/main/content/jcr_root/apps/academy/components/hello/ ├── .content.xml ← declares the component (cq:Component) └── hello.html ← the HTL script that renders it ``` The folder name (`hello`) is the component's name. AEM looks for a script named after the folder — `hello.html` — as the default renderer. ## Declare the component — `.content.xml` ```xml ``` - `cq:Component` — the node type that makes this a component. - `jcr:title` — the label shown in the **Insert New Component** dialog. - `componentGroup` — which group it belongs to. It must be **allowed by the template policy** to show up (covered in *Policies*). ## Render it — `hello.html` ```html

Hello, AEM!

``` That's a complete component. It renders **once it's deployed and placed on a page** — the next sections (and lessons) show how. ## How a page uses it Every component instance is a JCR node whose `sling:resourceType` points at the component: ```xml ``` AEM resolves `academy/components/hello` → the `/apps/academy/components/hello` folder → runs `hello.html`. This `resourceType` → script resolution is the heart of how AEM renders pages. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Page node
sling:resourceType =
academy/components/hello"] --> B["AEM searches
/apps then /libs"] B --> C["/apps/academy/components/hello"] C --> D["runs hello.html"] D --> E["HTML output"] ``` ## The resource-type search path AEM searches `/apps` first, then `/libs`. That's also how you **override or extend** out-of-the-box components: place a component at the same relative path under `/apps`, or point `sling:resourceSuperType` at a base component to inherit its scripts. One component = one responsibility. Compose pages from small components rather than building one giant one. ## Build, deploy & place it HTL is **not compiled locally** — your `.html` ships as-is and **AEM compiles it on the server** at render time. There's no build step for the markup itself. To get the component onto your local author (port 4502), run Maven from the project's **`code/`** folder: ```bash mvn clean install -PautoInstallSinglePackage ``` It lands at `/apps/academy/components/hello` (verify in CRX/DE). It only **renders once it's placed on a page** — two ways: - **Via the author UI** — drop it from *Insert New Component*. For that it must be **allowed by the template policy** (see *Policies*) and have a **dialog** (see *No Dialog* / *Dialog*). - **Directly in content** — add a node inside the page's **editable container**. On an editable template that's `jcr:content/root/responsivegrid` — **not `root` itself**. `root` is locked template structure (header, the responsivegrid, footer); nodes placed directly under `root` are ignored by the renderer and **won't show up**. ```xml ``` Common trap: putting the node directly under `root`. It must go in the **editable** container (`root/responsivegrid`), or it renders nothing — even though the node exists in CRX/DE. Newly created and nothing happens? It's almost always one of: not deployed (run the Maven command), placed in the wrong container (use `root/responsivegrid`), not **allowed** by the policy, or (to insert via the UI) it has **no dialog**. The next lessons cover each. ## What's next The following lessons add, layer by layer: a component **with no dialog**, then a **dialog** to let authors pass values, **clientlibs** for CSS/JS, **policies** for template-level configuration, and the **Style System** for author-selectable variations.

title: A Component with No Dialog
text:
Not every component needs a dialog. Plenty render **static markup** or content **derived from context** (the page, a service, the current user) rather than from author input. Start here — it's the simplest useful component. ## The simplest case: derived content, no authoring This component shows the current page's title using a **global object** — no dialog, no model: ```html

You are on: ${currentPage.title}

```

You are on: HTL Workshop

 ## Adding logic: a Sling Model (still no dialog) When you need real logic, put it in a **Sling Model** — a plain Java class annotated with `@Model`. It can read context without any authored field. ```java // core/src/main/java/biz/netcentric/academy/core/models/hello/PageMetaModel.java package biz.netcentric.academy.core.models.hello; import org.apache.sling.api.SlingHttpServletRequest; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.Model; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.DefaultInjectionStrategy; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.injectorspecific.ScriptVariable; import com.day.cq.wcm.api.Page; @Model(adaptables = SlingHttpServletRequest.class, defaultInjectionStrategy = DefaultInjectionStrategy.OPTIONAL) public class PageMetaModel { @ScriptVariable private Page currentPage; public String getBreadcrumbLabel() { // Logic lives in Java, not in HTL. return currentPage.getDepth() <= 2 ? "Home" : currentPage.getTitle(); } } ``` ```html ${meta.breadcrumbLabel} ``` - `@Model(adaptables = SlingHttpServletRequest.class)` — adapt the current **request** (required for `@ScriptVariable` to see the script bindings). - `@ScriptVariable` — inject a global object (here `currentPage`). It comes from the request's script context, so the model must adapt from the request, not the resource. - `data-sly-use` instantiates the model; `${meta.breadcrumbLabel}` calls `getBreadcrumbLabel()`. Even with no author input, keep branching/formatting in the model. HTL stays a thin view. ## The gotcha: showing up in the Insert dialog What a container offers in **Insert New Component** is decided by its **template policy** — your component's `componentGroup` must be in the allowed list (covered in *Policies*). That's the documented gate. Beyond that, a component an author can't configure is of limited use — and **in this project, a component with no `_cq_dialog` doesn't appear in the Insert dialog at all**. So if authors need to insert it by hand, give it a dialog (even a minimal/empty one). Missing from the Insert list? Check **both**: (1) its `componentGroup` is **allowed by the template policy**, and (2) it has a **`_cq_dialog`**. In this project, both gate whether it shows. A purely structural component placed by the template doesn't need either. ## When "no dialog" is the right choice - **Structure components** placed by the template (header, footer) and never inserted by hand. - Components **fully configured by policy** rather than per-instance content (see *Policies*). - Components that render **derived data** only (breadcrumb, current year, a service result). For everything an author should edit per instance, you need a dialog — next lesson.

title: Creating a Dialog to Pass Values
text:
A **dialog** gives authors a form to edit a component instance. Dialogs are built from **Granite UI** components and live in a `_cq_dialog` node next to the component. The value an author types is stored on the content node and read back by your model or HTL. ## The file ```text apps/academy/components/hello/ ├── .content.xml ├── hello.html └── _cq_dialog/ └── .content.xml ← the dialog definition ``` ## A minimal dialog ```xml ``` The crucial part is `name="./name"` — it stores the value as the `name` property on the component's content node. ## Reading the value Read it in a **Sling Model** with `@ValueMapValue` (the field name matches the property): ```java package biz.netcentric.academy.core.models.hello; import org.apache.sling.api.resource.Resource; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.Default; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.DefaultInjectionStrategy; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.Model; import org.apache.sling.models.annotations.injectorspecific.ValueMapValue; @Model(adaptables = Resource.class, defaultInjectionStrategy = DefaultInjectionStrategy.OPTIONAL) public class HelloModel { @ValueMapValue @Default(values = "world") private String name; public String getGreeting() { return "Hello, " + name + "!"; } } ``` This class lives in the **`core`** module. Organise models in a **per-component subpackage** — the project does this (`models/marquee/`, `models/hyperlink/`), so the `hello` component's model goes in a `hello` package. The folder path **must mirror the package / fully-qualified name** you pass to `data-sly-use`: ```text core/src/main/java/biz/netcentric/academy/core/models/hello/HelloModel.java └ package biz.netcentric.academy.core.models.hello; ← must match data-sly-use ``` The `package` line, the folder path, and the `data-sly-use` class name must all match exactly — a mismatch means `data-sly-use` can't find the model and the component errors. After adding or changing a model you must **rebuild & deploy `core`** (it's an OSGi bundle): `mvn clean install -PautoInstallSinglePackage` from `code/` (or `-PautoInstallBundle -pl core` for just the bundle). ```html

${hello.greeting}

``` With `name = "World"` authored in the dialog:

Hello, World!

 For trivial cases you can skip the model and read the property directly: `${properties.name}`. ## The full value flow ```mermaid flowchart TD D["Dialog field
name = ./name"] -->|author types World and saves| C["Content node
./name = World"] C -->|ValueMapValue injects it| M["Sling Model
getGreeting"] M -->|data-sly-use| H["HTL expression
hello.greeting"] H --> O["Hello, World!"] ``` ## Common field types | Granite resource type | Field | |---|---| | `…/form/textfield` | single-line text | | `…/form/textarea` | multi-line text | | `…/form/numberfield` | number | | `…/form/checkbox` | boolean | | `…/form/select` | dropdown | | `…/form/pathfield` | path picker (pages/assets) | A `numberfield` / `checkbox` left empty means the property is **null**. Guard with `@Default` in the model — an unguarded `${price > 100}` on a null value throws. ## Organising bigger dialogs Wrap fields in **tabs** (`granite/ui/components/coral/foundation/tabs`) and **fixed columns** once you have more than a handful — see the project's `marquee` component for a multi-tab, multifield example. Always add `fieldDescription`s — authors rely on them, and they document the component.

title: Adding the Clientlibs
text:
A component usually needs CSS (and sometimes JS). AEM delivers these through a **client library** (clientlib) — a folder of CSS/JS that AEM bundles, minifies, and serves under a **category** name you reference from HTL. ## Where styles come from in this project The front end lives in `ui.frontend`. You author SCSS per component: ```text ui.frontend/src/main/frontend/academy/publish/components/hello/ └── hello.clientlibs.scss ``` The build (`nc-fe-build`) compiles it into a generated clientlib whose **category** follows the folder path: `academy.publish.components.hello`. A clientlib is generated **only for components that have a source file here**. A markup-only component (like the `hello` from earlier lessons, with no `.clientlibs.scss`/`.js`) produces **no clientlib** — that's expected, not an error. Add a source file only when the component actually needs styling or behaviour. ## Loading the clientlib from HTL Pull the category in with the Granite `clientlib` template: ```html

${hello.greeting}

``` - `clientlib.all` injects both CSS and JS. There are also `clientlib.css` and `clientlib.js` if you need only one. - The `categories` string must match the generated category exactly. ## Author mode & the decoration tag A component is **edited in author mode** and **viewed in publish** — the DOM and the needs differ. - **The decoration tag** — AEM usually wraps your component in a `
` (the *decoration tag*) carrying its CSS class and edit hooks, so your markup isn't always the outermost element. Style your **own** root class (`.hello`), not "the wrapper". Control it per component in `.content.xml` with `cq:noDecoration="{Boolean}true"` (no wrapper) or `cq:htmlTag`. The **Style System** adds the author-selected class to this tag automatically (see *Policies*). - **Author-only styling** — load edit affordances (outlines, "empty" hints) behind a `wcmmode` guard so they never ship to publish: ```html ``` The matching SCSS lives under `academy/author/…`, so the build emits an `academy.author.*` category loaded only in edit mode. In author mode your component sits inside editor chrome (overlays, the decoration wrapper). Don't assume it's the outermost element or rely on `:first-child`/`:last-child` against the page. ## Adding JavaScript — the component loader CSS is declarative; for **behaviour** you add a JS file beside the component and wire it up with the [Netcentric component loader](https://github.com/Netcentric/component-loader). You never call your code directly — you **register** a class, and the loader creates an instance for every matching element on the page (including ones authors add live). ### 1 · Write the component's JS The source sits next to the SCSS: ```text ui.frontend/src/main/frontend/academy/publish/components/hello/ ├── hello.clientlibs.scss └── hello.clientlibs.js ``` ```js import { register } from '@netcentric/component-loader'; class Hello { constructor(element, options) { this.element = element; // the DOM node carrying data-nc="Hello" this.options = options; // parsed from data-nc-params-Hello (JSON) this.init(); } init() { // Scope queries to this.element — never document.querySelector. this.element.addEventListener('click', () => { this.element.classList.toggle('hello--active'); }); } } register({ Hello }); // the key "Hello" is the name you use in data-nc export default Hello; ``` ### 2 · Make the build aware of it Export it from the publish entry so the bundler includes it — if it's never imported, it's never registered and nothing runs: ```js // publish/components/index.js export * from 'academy-base-project/publish/components/hello/hello'; ``` ### 3 · The loader runtime (set up once per page) One entry boots the loader: `run()` initialises components already in the DOM, and `observe()` initialises any added afterwards (essential for author mode): ```js // publish.clientlibs.js import { observe, run } from '@netcentric/component-loader'; run(); // defaults to scanning document for the data-nc attribute observe(); ``` ### 4 · Wire it from HTL Mark the element with `data-nc` (the **registered name** — case-sensitive). Optionally pass config as **valid JSON** in `data-nc-params-`; it arrives as the constructor's `options`: ```html

${hello.greeting}

``` ### Loader features worth knowing - **Multiple components on one element:** `data-nc="Hello,Tracker"` (+ a `data-nc-params-…` per name). - **Lazy init:** `data-nc-loading="lazy"` defers instantiation until the element scrolls into view — ideal for heavy or below-the-fold components. - **Introspection:** the loader exports `components` (registered classes) and `instances` (live instances by name). The loader ships **untranspiled**, so the front-end build must run Babel over it — include `@netcentric` in `babel-loader` rather than blanket-excluding `node_modules`. `nc-fe-build` already handles this; it only bites in a hand-rolled webpack config. This is the component-author's view. For the behaviour itself — events, delegation, custom events, params/DOM references, and performance patterns — see the **JavaScript track**. ## The manual alternative — `cq:ClientLibraryFolder` Without the FE build you can define a clientlib node by hand under the component (or under `/apps/academy/clientlibs`): ```xml ``` with sibling files listing what to include: ```text hello-clientlib/ ├── .content.xml ← cq:ClientLibraryFolder (above) ├── css.txt ← #base=css → hello.css ├── js.txt ← #base=js → hello.js ├── css/hello.css └── js/hello.js ``` The FE build just automates generating this from your SCSS/JS sources. ## Useful clientlib properties | Property | Purpose | |---|---| | `categories` | the name(s) you load by | | `allowProxy` | serve under `/etc.clientlibs` (recommended, keeps `/apps` private) | | `dependencies` | other categories that must load first | | `embed` | inline another category's output into this one | Name categories by environment + type + component (`academy.publish.components.hello`). Consistent naming keeps the clientlib graph predictable as the project grows. Next: how **policies** configure components per template — including which clientlibs and which **Style System** variations are available.

title: Policies
text:
A **policy** is design-time configuration attached to a component **within an editable
template**. Where a *dialog* edits one component instance's content, a *policy* configures a
component for **every instance** on pages built from that template.

<Callout type="note">**Dialog vs Policy.** Dialog = per-instance *content*, edited by the page author. Policy = per-template *configuration*, set once by the template author and applied everywhere.</Callout>

## Where policies live

Editable templates and their policies live under `/conf`:

```text
/conf/academy/settings/wcm/
├── templates/academy-homepage/
│   ├── structure/      ← locked layout + which components are where
│   ├── initial/        ← content a new page starts with
│   └── policies/       ← maps each component → a policy
└── policies/           ← the policy definitions themselves
```

## Use 1 — Allowed components (why your component shows up)

The responsive grid's policy lists which **component groups** an author may insert. This is
exactly what decides whether your new component appears in **Insert New Component**:

```xml
<policy_default_component_parsys
    jcr:primaryType="nt:unstructured"
    jcr:title="Academy Default Parsys Policy"
    sling:resourceType="wcm/core/components/policy/policy"
    components="group:Academy"/>
```

<Callout type="warn">If a component is missing from the Insert dialog, its `componentGroup` is probably not in this list. Add the group (e.g. `group:Academy`) or list the component path explicitly, then redeploy the policy.</Callout>

## Use 2 — Configuring a component via its design dialog

A component can expose **policy-level options** through a **design dialog**
(`cq:design_dialog`, same Granite UI as a normal dialog). The template author sets them once;
the values are stored in the policy and read at render time.

```text
apps/academy/components/title/
├── _cq_dialog/         ← per-instance content (the title text)
└── _cq_design_dialog/  ← per-policy options (allowed heading sizes, default style…)
```

Read policy values in HTL through the `currentStyle` global object:

```html
<sly data-sly-test.allowH1="${currentStyle.allowH1}">…</sly>
${currentStyle.defaultColor}
```

…or in a Sling Model with `@ScriptVariable private Style currentStyle;`.

## How a template wires it together

The template's `policies` node maps each component to a policy by `cq:policy`:

```xml
<root
    cq:policy="wcm/foundation/components/responsivegrid/policy_default_container"
    jcr:primaryType="nt:unstructured"
    sling:resourceType="wcm/core/components/policies/mapping">
  <image
      cq:policy="academy/components/image/policy_image"
      jcr:primaryType="nt:unstructured"
      sling:resourceType="wcm/core/components/policies/mapping"/>
</root>
```

Here the `image` component is bound to a policy that fixes its rendition widths, crop ratios,
and lazy-loading — author-proof configuration that applies to every image on the template.

## Editing policies in the UI

In practice template authors do this visually: **Templates → open template → Structure mode →
select a component → Policy tab**. The XML above is what that UI writes to `/conf`.

<Callout type="do">Decide deliberately: does a setting belong to the **author of each instance** (dialog) or to the **template** (policy)? Heading-level *options*, allowed components, and style variations are policy; the actual heading *text* is dialog.</Callout>

title: Style System
text:
The **Style System** lets authors switch between predefined visual variations of a component by toggling CSS classes — **without a code change per variation and without a new dialog field**. It's a policy feature: you define named styles in the component's policy, AEM adds the chosen class to the component's wrapper, and your clientlib provides the CSS. ## The three pieces ```text 1. Policy defines the styles (label → CSS class) → cq:styleGroups 2. AEM adds the selected class to the wrapper → automatic 3. Your clientlib defines what that class looks like → BEM modifier in SCSS ``` ## 1 · Define styles in the policy Styles live under `cq:styleGroups` in the component's policy. Each style has a label (what the author sees) and the class(es) it applies: ```xml ``` In the UI this is the **Styles** tab of the component's policy. The author then picks a style from the component toolbar on the page. ## 2 · AEM applies the class When an author selects "Hero", AEM adds `title--hero` to the component's wrapping element automatically. You don't read anything in HTL — the class is just there on the wrapper: ```html
… your component markup …
``` ## 3 · Style the classes in your clientlib (BEM) Your job is to make those classes mean something. The project's stylelint config enforces **BEM** (`block__element--modifier`, kebab-case): ```scss $module-name: "title"; .#{$module-name} { &__text { font-weight: 700; } &--hero { // .title--hero font-size: 3rem; text-transform: uppercase; } &--compact { // .title--compact font-size: 1.25rem; opacity: .8; } } ``` Component toolbar → **Styles** → *Hero* / *Compact* The same component, two policy-driven looks — no new dialog field, no code change.

Hero title

Compact title

 ## Style System vs a dialog dropdown You *could* add a `select` dialog field and a `${'title--' @ format=...}` class instead — but: Use the **Style System** for purely visual variations (appearance, spacing, theme). It keeps presentation in policy + CSS, needs no model code, and gives authors a consistent picker across all components. Don't hand-roll a "style" dialog dropdown for what the Style System already does — you'd duplicate a platform feature and add model/HTL code to maintain. ## You've now covered the full path **Structure → no-dialog → dialog → clientlibs → policies → Style System.** That's everything to build, configure, and skin an AEM component the way this project does. Advanced next steps: multifield lists, extending Core Components via `sling:resourceSuperType`, and unit-testing the Sling Model with AEM Mocks.

title: What is Context-Aware Configuration?
text:
**Context-Aware Configuration** (CAC) lets configuration vary by *where you are in the content tree* — so a brand, region or site can each have different settings, chosen automatically by the page's location. ## The problem it solves OSGi configuration is **global per instance** (optionally per run mode). But "the social-media links", "the API key for this brand", or "is the newsletter signup on?" should differ **per site**, set by authors/admins — not by a developer redeploying. CAC resolves configuration by walking up from the current resource to find the nearest configured value. ## How context is established A content tree is associated with a configuration bucket via a **`sling:configRef`** property (usually on the site root), pointing into **`/conf`**: ```text /content/wknd → sling:configRef = /conf/wknd /conf/wknd/sling:configs/... ← the actual config values live here ``` Resolve a config for `/content/wknd/en/home` and CAC finds `/conf/wknd`'s values. A sub-site can point at its own `/conf` and **override**. ```mermaid flowchart TD Page["/content/wknd/en/home"] -->|"walk up for nearest sling:configRef"| Root["/content/wknd
sling:configRef = /conf/wknd"] Root --> Conf["/conf/wknd
config values"] Conf --> Val["resolved SiteConfig"] ``` ## CAC vs OSGi config | | OSGi config | Context-Aware config | |---|---|---| | Scope | whole instance (or run mode) | per content context (`/conf`) | | Edited by | developers (deploy) / admins (console) | authors/admins, per site | | Lives in | `/apps` config / console | `/conf` | | Use for | endpoints, technical settings | per-site/brand business settings | Reach for CAC when a setting should differ **per site/brand/region** and be owned by authors. Keep technical, instance-wide settings (timeouts, endpoints) in **OSGi** config.

title: The CAC API
Unit-testing CAC
The wcm.io CAC Editor
text:
The raw `/conf` nodes aren't author-friendly. The **wcm.io Context-Aware Configuration Editor** gives
admins a touch-UI to edit your configs without touching CRXDE.

## Install it

Add the wcm.io CAC editor packages (and their dependencies) to your project — embedded in the `all`
package, or installed via Package Manager:

- `io.wcm.caconfig.editor` — the touch-UI editor
- `io.wcm.caconfig.extensions` — AEM integration (resolves `/conf`, persistence)

Once installed it appears under **Tools → Configuration Browser / Context-Aware Configuration**, or per
page via the editor URL.

## Use it

1. Create a **Configuration Browser** container for your context (Tools → General → Configuration Browser) and enable *Context-Aware Configuration* on it — this provisions `/conf/<name>`.
2. Set the site root's `sling:configRef` to that `/conf/<name>`.
3. Open the **CAC Editor**, pick the context, and you'll see *your* `@Configuration` interfaces rendered as forms — fill in `brandName`, toggle `newsletterEnabled`, add `socialLinks`.
4. Save — values persist under `/conf/<name>/sling:configs/...` and your model reads them immediately.

## Why it matters

Your `@Property(label = ...)` annotations become the **field labels** in the editor — so a well-annotated
config is self-documenting for the admins who fill it in.

<Callout type="do">Label and describe every config property. The editor renders those labels, so good annotations turn your config into a clean admin form — no CRXDE, no developer round-trip to change a per-site setting.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">The Configuration Browser provisions the `/conf` bucket and the `sling:configRef` wiring; the wcm.io editor edits the values inside it. You need both: the bucket (context) and the editor (values).</Callout>

title: Assignment: Per-site Config with CAC
text:
Give the academy site author-editable, per-context settings.

## 1 — Define the config

- [ ] Create a `SiteConfig` `@Configuration` interface (e.g. `brandName`, `newsletterEnabled`, `socialLinks[]`) with labels + defaults

## 2 — Read it

- [ ] In a `FooterModel`, resolve `SiteConfig` via `ConfigurationBuilder` for the current resource
- [ ] Render `brandName` in the footer HTL

## 3 — Edit it

- [ ] Provision a `/conf/academy` context (Configuration Browser) and set `sling:configRef` on the site root
- [ ] Install the wcm.io CAC editor and set `brandName` through the form; confirm the footer updates

## 4 — Override & test

- [ ] Create a sub-site context with a different `brandName`; confirm the nearest context wins
- [ ] Unit-test the model with the CAC test support: assert the configured value and the default fallback

<Callout type="do">Done when the footer's brand name comes from `/conf`, an admin can change it in the editor, a sub-site overrides it, and a unit test pins both the resolved value and the default.</Callout>

title: What Copilot Is (and Isn\
text:
GitHub Copilot is an **AI pair programmer** built into your editor. It doesn't "know" your project —
it predicts the most likely next code given the **context it can see** (your open files, selection,
and what you tell it). Treat it as a fast, well-read junior who needs review, not an oracle.

## The three ways you'll use it

<Callout type="note">These overlap, but it helps to know which one you're invoking — each gets a different amount of context and control.</Callout>

| Mode | What it does | Best for |
|---|---|---|
| **Completions** (ghost text) | Suggests the next line(s) as you type | Boilerplate, repetitive code, the "obvious" next step |
| **Chat** | A conversation in a side panel or inline | Explaining, debugging, generating a block, asking "how do I…" |
| **Edits / Agent** | Multi-file changes from a single instruction | Refactors, "add a field to this component end-to-end" |

## How it actually works (the mental model)

Copilot sends a **prompt** — assembled from your nearby code, open tabs, and your message — to a
large language model, which returns the statistically likely continuation. Two consequences follow
that shape everything in this module:

- **Context quality = output quality.** Garbage or missing context → confident but wrong code.
- **It predicts plausible, not correct.** It will happily invent an API method that doesn't exist
  (a *hallucination*). That's why AEM — with its niche APIs — needs extra care (lessons 05–06).

<Callout type="warn">Copilot does not run your code, read your runtime, or check AEM's Javadoc. A suggestion that *looks* right can reference a method that was removed, a Granite resource type that doesn't exist, or an HTL block option that was never valid. **You** are the compiler-of-last-resort.</Callout>

## The one habit that makes Copilot safe

**Read every suggestion before you accept it.** The developers who get burned are the ones who
tab-accept on autopilot; the ones who get a real speed-up read the diff, keep what's right, and
re-prompt for the rest. We'll build that habit throughout the module.

<Callout type="do">Think of Copilot as **draft-generation + review**, never **generate-and-ship**. Your value shifts from typing to specifying and verifying.</Callout>

## What's next

Next: getting it installed and learning the interfaces — completions, the Chat panel, and inline
chat — so the rest of the module has something to point at.

title: Setup & the Interfaces
text:
A five-minute setup, then a tour of the surfaces you'll live in. Copilot works in **VS Code**,
**JetBrains (IntelliJ / WebStorm)**, and others — the concepts are identical; the keybindings differ.

## Install & sign in

- [ ] Install the **GitHub Copilot** extension (and **GitHub Copilot Chat**) from your IDE's marketplace
- [ ] Sign in with the GitHub account that has a Copilot license (your Netcentric org seat)
- [ ] Confirm the status-bar Copilot icon shows **active** (not a slash through it)

<Callout type="note">JetBrains users: install the *GitHub Copilot* plugin from Settings → Plugins. Same features, IntelliJ-style keybindings.</Callout>

## Completions — the ghost text

As you type, Copilot shows a grey suggestion. The defaults in VS Code:

| Action | Shortcut |
|---|---|
| Accept suggestion | `Tab` |
| Accept one word | `Cmd/Ctrl + →` |
| Next / previous suggestion | `Alt/Option + ]` / `[` |
| Dismiss | `Esc` |
| Trigger manually | `Alt/Option + \` |

The strongest trigger is a **clear lead-in**: a function signature, a descriptive variable name, or a
comment describing intent. Write the *what*, let it draft the *how*.

## Chat — the conversation

Open the **Chat panel** (sidebar) for back-and-forth: explanations, "how do I…", generating a block,
debugging. It can see your open editor and selection, and you steer it with context references
(lesson 04).

**Inline chat** (`Cmd/Ctrl + I` in VS Code) opens a prompt *right in the file* — ideal for "refactor
this selection" or "add a null guard here" without leaving your code.

## Slash commands

In Chat (panel or inline), `/` invokes a focused action. The ones you'll use most:

| Command | Does |
|---|---|
| `/explain` | Explain the selected code |
| `/fix` | Propose a fix for an error or smell |
| `/tests` | Generate unit tests for the selection |
| `/doc` | Add doc comments |
| `/new` | Scaffold a new file/project from a description |

<Callout type="do">Spend ten minutes pressing every shortcut on real code before reading on. Muscle memory for *accept word*, *next suggestion*, and *inline chat* is where the daily speed-up actually comes from.</Callout>

## What's next

You now have the surfaces. The skill that separates fast users from frustrated ones is **prompting** —
that's the next lesson.

title: Writing Effective Prompts
text: The single biggest lever on Copilot's usefulness is **how you ask**. A vague prompt gets a vague, generic answer; a specific one — with constraints, examples, and the surrounding context — gets code you can almost keep as-is. ## Be specific: say the what, the constraints, and the shape A weak prompt and a strong prompt for the *same* task: ```text make a function to format a date ``` You'll get *a* date formatter — probably not your format, locale, or input type. ```text Write a JS function formatPublishDate(isoString) that takes an ISO 8601 string and returns "12 Jun 2026" (en-GB, day-month-year). Use Intl.DateTimeFormat. Return an empty string for null/invalid input. No external libraries. ``` Name, input, exact output, the API to use, the edge case, and a constraint — little left to guess. ## Comments as prompts (the completions workflow) For ghost-text completions, a precise comment above the cursor is your prompt. The more it reads like a spec, the better the draft: ```js // Debounce: return a function that calls fn at most once per `wait` ms, // firing on the trailing edge. Cancel the pending call on each new invocation. function debounce(fn, wait) { // Copilot drafts the body from the comment above } ``` ## Give it an example (one-shot prompting) When you want a specific *style*, show one. Copilot mirrors patterns: ```text Here's how we write a Sling Model getter: @ValueMapValue @Default(values = "") private String title; public String getTitle() { return title; } Now add the same pattern for a `linkUrl` String and a `newTab` boolean. ``` ## Iterate — don't restart The first answer is a draft. Refine it in place instead of re-typing the whole request: - *"Good — now handle the null case and add JSDoc."* - *"Use `const`/arrow functions to match our style."* - *"That method doesn't exist on this API — use `getValueMap()` instead."* Treat the conversation as a feedback loop: prompt → read → correct → re-prompt. Three quick iterations beat one giant prompt almost every time. ## A reusable prompt skeleton When a request matters, fill in: Goal (what), Inputs/Outputs (signatures, exact shape), Constraints (libraries, style, perf), Edge cases, and an Example if style matters. You rarely need all five — but naming them stops you writing "make a thing". ## What's next Prompts are only half the picture — Copilot also needs the right **context**. Next: how it sees your code, and how to point it at exactly the right files.

title: Context & Custom Instructions
text: Copilot answers from the **context** it's given. Completions read your nearby code and open tabs; Chat reads your selection plus whatever you reference. Learning to *steer* that context is what makes answers project-aware instead of generic. ## What Copilot can see - **Completions:** the current file around your cursor, and (to a degree) other **open tabs**. Keeping the relevant file open is the cheapest way to improve suggestions. - **Chat:** your active editor + current selection by default, plus anything you explicitly attach. Before asking Chat about a component, open its files — the HTL, the Sling Model, the dialog. Relevant tabs open = sharper, project-specific answers. ## Context references in Chat In VS Code, attach precise context with `#` and direct the request with `@`: | Reference | Pulls in | |---|---| | `#file` | A specific file you pick | | `#selection` | The current editor selection | | `#codebase` (or `@workspace`) | Lets Copilot search the whole project for relevant code | | `#changes` | Your current git diff | | `@workspace` | A participant that reasons across the repo | | `@terminal` | Help with the last terminal command/output | Example: ```text @workspace where is the component-loader registered, and how do existing components export themselves into the publish bundle? Show the pattern. ``` That answer is grounded in *your* repo, not a generic template — exactly what you want for AEM. ## Custom instructions — teach Copilot your conventions once Drop a **`.github/copilot-instructions.md`** at the repo root and Copilot Chat will read it on every request. This is the highest-leverage setup step for an AEM project, because it injects the conventions Copilot can't infer: ```markdown # Project conventions for Copilot - This is an AEM Maven project. Front end lives in `ui.frontend` (SCSS + component-loader JS). - HTL files use Sightly; prefer `data-sly-use` with Sling Models over inline logic. - JS components are classes registered via `@netcentric/component-loader`; scope DOM queries to `this.element`. - Styles use BEM and `@use` (never `@import`); read tokens from the shared maps. - Sling Models: `@Model(adaptables = …)`, `@ValueMapValue`, package per component under `…core.models.`. - Never invent AEM/Granite APIs — if unsure, say so. ``` You can also add task-specific files under .github/instructions/ and per-prompt files (*.prompt.md) for repeatable workflows. Start with the single root file — it already changes the quality of every answer. Custom instructions guide, they don't guarantee. Copilot can still ignore a rule or hallucinate an API. The review habit from lesson 01 still applies to every suggestion. ## What's next With prompting and context under your belt, the next two lessons get concrete — Copilot for AEM **front-end**, then **full-stack**.

title: Copilot for AEM Front-End
text:
This is where it gets practical for FE devs. Copilot is excellent at the **standard web** parts of an AEM front end (SCSS, vanilla JS, BEM) and **weakest** at the AEM-specific glue (HTL syntax, Granite, clientlib categories) — because there's far less of it in its training data. Lean on it for the former; verify hard on the latter. ## HTL — useful, but check the syntax Copilot knows HTML well, so HTL markup comes out mostly right — but it sometimes invents block options or mixes in JSP/Thymeleaf habits. Prompt with the model in mind: ```text Write an HTL snippet that uses a Sling Model `hero` (data-sly-use) to render an

title and an optional

teaser. Use data-sly-test to skip the teaser when empty. No inline business logic. ``` Verify HTL block expressions (data-sly-test, data-sly-list, data-sly-resource) and the display-context/escaping options against the official HTL spec. A plausible-looking @ context= value or list option may not exist. ## SCSS & BEM — Copilot's home turf Styling is where you'll get the biggest, safest speed-up. Give it your conventions and it produces clean BEM partials: ```text Write a BEM SCSS partial for a `.card` component (block, __media, __title, __body, --featured modifier). Use @use to read $spacing and $brand from our tokens, keep nesting ≤2 levels, animate only transform/opacity on hover. ``` It's also great at the boring-but-error-prone bits: converting a hex palette to custom properties, generating a spacing scale with `@each`, or writing a responsive `clamp()` type ramp. ## Component-loader JS — give it the pattern Copilot won't know the `@netcentric/component-loader` conventions unless you show it (or it's in your `copilot-instructions.md`). Once it has the pattern, it scaffolds reliably: ```text Following our component-loader pattern (a class taking (element, options), register({ Name }), DOM queries scoped to this.element, idempotent init), write a `Tabs` component that uses event delegation and reads an initial open index from this.options. ``` Keep an existing component open in another tab when you ask. Copilot mirrors the open file's structure — your real register() + class shape — far more accurately than a from-scratch guess. ## Where it stumbles (so you don't trust it blindly) - **Clientlib categories & `cq:ClientLibraryFolder`** — it may guess category names or `allowProxy` wrong; check against your actual generated categories. - **Granite UI dialogs** — resource types like `granite/ui/components/coral/foundation/form/textfield` are often *almost* right; one wrong segment and the field won't render. Copy known-good resource types. - **HTL global objects** — it can confuse `properties`, `pageProperties`, `currentPage`, and `wcmmode` usage. Rule of thumb: the closer a task is to "normal web dev", the more you can trust the draft. The closer it is to "AEM plumbing", the more you verify against docs or a known-good example in the repo. ## What's next Front-end covered — next, the full-stack side: Sling Models, servlets, dialogs, and tests.

title: Copilot for Full-Stack AEM
text: For full-stack devs, Copilot shines at **Java boilerplate** — Sling Models, getters, builders, DTOs — and at the repetitive parts of OSGi and tests. It's shakier on AEM/Sling **API surface** and on hand-written **JCR XML**. Same principle as the front end: trust the boilerplate, verify the plumbing. ## Sling Models — fast and mostly right Models are pattern-heavy, so Copilot scaffolds them well. Show it the annotations you use: ```text Write a Sling Model `HeroModel` adaptable from Resource, OPTIONAL injection. Inject `title` (String), `teaser` (String), and `ctaUrl` (String) via @ValueMapValue with @Default. Add a getCtaTarget() returning "_blank" when a `newTab` boolean is true, else "_self". Package: …core.models.hero. ``` Be explicit about adaptables — Resource.class for value-map injection, SlingHttpServletRequest.class when you need @ScriptVariable bindings like currentPage. Copilot often defaults to one and you want the other. ## Servlets & OSGi — good skeletons, check the annotations Copilot writes solid `SlingSafeMethodsServlet` / `@Component` skeletons, but **verify the OSGi R7 annotations and registration properties** — `@SlingServletResourceTypes` vs `@SlingServletPaths`, service rankings, config PIDs. These are exactly the details it half-remembers. ```text Write a SlingSafeMethodsServlet registered by resourceType for a GET that returns JSON of a page's child titles. Use @SlingServletResourceTypes with methods=GET and extensions=json. Guard nulls. ``` ## Granite dialogs (`.content.xml`) — generate, then fix the resource types Copilot can draft a dialog's XML structure, but **the `sling:resourceType` strings are the failure point** — one wrong path and the field silently doesn't render. Generate the shape, then paste your known-good resource types over the guesses. Hand-written JCR XML (dialogs, clientlib folders, content nodes) is where hallucinations hurt most because there's no compiler to catch them — the node just doesn't behave. Always cross-check against a working node in the repo. ## Tests — a genuine time-saver This is one of the best ROI uses for full-stack AEM. With **AEM Mocks (`io.wcm.testing` / `aem-mock`)** or `sling-mock`, Copilot generates the ceremony around `context.create.resource(...)`, `context.currentResource(...)`, and `context.request().adaptTo(...)` quickly: ```text Using AemContext (aem-mock, JUnit 5), write a test for HeroModel: create a resource with title/teaser/newTab props, adaptTo(HeroModel.class), and assert getCtaTarget() returns "_blank" when newTab is true and "_self" otherwise. ``` Select the class under test, then run /tests with a note like "use AemContext and cover the null/default branches". You review and tighten — much faster than writing the harness by hand. ## What's next You've got the AEM-specific uses. Next: the day-to-day **Chat workflows** — explain, fix, refactor, debug — that tie it all together.

title: Everyday Chat Workflows
text: Beyond completions, most daily value comes from a handful of **Chat workflows**. Here are the ones worth making automatic, each shown the way you'd actually use it on an AEM codebase. ## Understand unfamiliar code — `/explain` Inherited a gnarly Sling Model or a dense HTL block? Select it and ask: ```text /explain — and call out any AEM-specific behaviour (injection, adaptation, HTL block options) a newcomer would miss. ``` Great for onboarding into a client project and for reviewing a teammate's PR before you comment. ## Fix an error — `/fix` Paste the stack trace or compiler error (or just select the broken code): ```text /fix — NullPointerException in getCtaTarget when `newTab` is unset. Keep it null-safe with @Default rather than an if-ladder. ``` Read *why* it proposes the fix, not just the patch — that's where you catch a fix that hides the symptom instead of solving it. ## Refactor safely Inline chat on a selection, with the constraint stated: ```text Refactor this to extract the URL-building into a private method. Behaviour must stay identical. Don't change the public getters. ``` After any refactor, run the tests (or ask /tests first, refactor second).
