<Callout type="do">Done when your `HashSet`/`TreeSet` behave as the contracts predict, your formatter splits cleanly into interface + abstract base, your stream pipeline reads top-to-bottom, and you've stepped through live AEM code with a conditional breakpoint.</Callout>

title: JavaScript Basics
text:
JavaScript adds interactivity to a website. The language core is compact; on top of it sit browser APIs, third-party APIs, and frameworks. ## Variables & constants Declare with `let` or `const` (avoid `var`). Assign, then optionally reassign. ```js let name = 'John'; // declare + assign name = 'Bob'; // ok const id = 42; // can't be reassigned // id = 7; // TypeError const list = []; // the binding is constant, the contents aren't list.push('hello'); // ['hello'] ← fine ``` Default to `const`; use `let` only when you must reassign. Never `var`. ## Scope - **Global** — accessible everywhere - **Function** — visible only inside the function - **Block** — `let`/`const` inside `{ }` (if, for, while) are visible only in that block **Lexical scope:** an inner function can read variables from its enclosing scope. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph G["Global scope"] subgraph F["Function scope"] subgraph B["Block scope — if / for / while"] V["let / const declared here
visible only inside the block"] end end end ``` Inner scopes can read outward; outer scopes cannot read in. ```js const name = 'John'; function outer() { const surname = 'Wick'; // inner can see name + surname return `${name} ${surname}`; } // surname is NOT visible out here ``` ## Data types A few **primitives**, plus **objects** for everything structured: ```js const title = 'Hello'; // String — text const price = 42; // Number — ints & floats (one number type) const inStock = true; // Boolean — true / false const nothing = null; // null — an intentional "no value" let notSet; // undefined — declared but not assigned const tags = ['new', 'sale']; // Array — an ordered list const user = { name: 'Ada', age: 36 }; // Object — key/value pairs ``` Everything that isn't a primitive is an **object** — arrays, functions, and DOM nodes included. Check a value's type with `typeof`: ```js typeof title; // 'string' typeof price; // 'number' typeof inStock; // 'boolean' typeof notSet; // 'undefined' typeof tags; // 'object' (arrays are objects) typeof user; // 'object' typeof null; // 'object' (a long-standing quirk) ``` ## Operators ```js 6 + 9; // 15 (also string concat: 'a' + 'b') 20 / 2; // 10 let n = 4; n === 3; // false (strict equality) n !== 3; // true ``` ## Control flow & loops ```js if (food === 'ice cream') { /* … */ } else { /* … */ } for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) { /* runs 5 times */ } ``` Also: `switch`, `while`, `do…while`, `for…of` (values), `for…in` (keys), and `return` / `break` / `continue`. ## Functions ```js function sum(a, b) { return a + b; } sum(3, 5); // 8 ``` ## Events (a first look) ```js const button = document.querySelector('.button'); button.addEventListener('click', () => console.log('clicked')); ``` Run it yourself — **click the button** and watch the console: Click me`} js={`const button = document.querySelector('.btn'); let count = 0; button.addEventListener('click', () => { count += 1; console.log('clicked ' + count + ' time(s)'); });`} /> These fundamentals power everything that follows. Next up: **classes, `this` & `bind`** — the backbone of the Netcentric component pattern.

title: Classes, this & bind
Functional Components
Modern JavaScript
text:
The most useful recent additions to the language — each shown **before → after** so the win is clear. ## Optional chaining `?.` Short-circuits to `undefined` instead of throwing when part of the chain is missing. **Before** ```js const city = person && person.address && person.address.city; ``` **After** ```js const city = person?.address?.city; onChange?.(); // call only if it exists ``` Reading data that might be missing — API responses, optional config, optional callbacks. ## Logical assignment `||=` `??=` Assign only when a condition holds — great for defaults and lazy init. **Before** ```js if (!opts.mode) opts.mode = 'auto'; this.cache = this.cache || new Map(); ``` **After** ```js opts.mode ||= 'auto'; // set if falsy this.cache ??= new Map(); // set only if null/undefined ``` Apply a default, or lazy-init a value the first time it's needed. ## Dynamic import & top-level await Load code only when it's actually needed, and `await` directly at module top level. **Before** ```js import Chart from './chart.js'; // always in the bundle ``` **After** ```js const { default: Chart } = await import('./chart.js'); // loaded on demand ``` Defer a heavy or rarely-used dependency out of the initial bundle — a key performance lever. ## Promise helpers **Before** ```js const [a, b] = await Promise.all([p1, p2]); // rejects if either one fails ``` **After** ```js await Promise.allSettled([p1, p2]); // every result, success or failure await Promise.any([p1, p2]); // first success wins ``` `allSettled` when you need **every** result even if some fail; `any` for **first success wins** (e.g. fallback endpoints). ## Arrays & strings **Before** ```js arr[arr.length - 1]; // last item [...arr].sort(); // copy first — .sort() mutates the original ``` **After** ```js arr.at(-1); // last item arr.toSorted(); // sorted copy, original untouched str.replaceAll('+', ' '); // every occurrence, no global regex ``` `.at(-1)` for the last item; the immutable `toSorted` / `toReversed` / `with` when you must not mutate shared state. ## ES modules **Before (CommonJS)** ```js const { add } = require('./math.js'); module.exports = { add }; ``` **After (ES modules)** ```js import add, { name } from './math.js'; export const name = 'Ada'; // named export export default function add(a, b) {} // default export ``` Named exports for a set of utilities; a default export for a module's one main thing; re-exports to build a barrel (`index.js`). Tweak the values and watch the console: Open the console below 👇

`} js={`const user = { name: 'Ada' }; // no 'age' property console.log(user?.address?.city); // undefined — no error console.log(user.age ?? 18); // 18 console.log([3, 1, 2].toSorted()); // [1, 2, 3] console.log([10, 20, 30].at(-1)); // 30`} /> `?.` removes most defensive `if (x && x.y)` checks. Reach for `toSorted` / `with` when you must not mutate shared data.

title: Events & Event Delegation
Arrays & Data Transformation
text:
Most JS work is reshaping arrays of data. A handful of methods cover almost everything — and they **return new values** instead of mutating, which suits the functional style. ## The core methods | Method | Returns | For | |---|---|---| | `map` | a new array, same length | transform each item | | `filter` | a new, shorter array | keep items that pass a test | | `reduce` | one value | fold a list into a total/object | | `find` | the first match (or `undefined`) | look one up | | `some` / `every` | a boolean | "any?" / "all?" | They **chain** — each returns something the next can work on: ```js const names = products .filter((p) => p.inStock) .map((p) => p.name); ``` ## Keep it immutable `map`/`filter` already return copies. For sorting, use `toSorted()` (not `sort()`, which mutates), and spread to copy before changing: ```js const cheapestFirst = [...products].sort((a, b) => a.price - b.price); // copy, then sort ``` ## Practice Open the console and implement each requirement: Open the console 👇

`} js={`const products = [ { name: 'Pen', price: 2, inStock: true }, { name: 'Mug', price: 8, inStock: false }, { name: 'Cap', price: 12, inStock: true } ]; // 1. Log the names of the in-stock products, comma-separated // 2. Log the total price of the in-stock products // 3. Log whether any in-stock product costs more than 10 // 4. Log the name of the first product cheaper than 5 `} /> Reach for `map`/`filter`/`reduce` before a `for` loop — they say *what* you want, return new data, and chain cleanly.

title: Closures & Higher-Order Functions
Pure Functions & Immutability
text:
## Pure functions

A function is **pure** when:

1. The same input always gives the same output.
2. It has **no side effects** — it doesn't change anything outside itself (globals, the DOM, its arguments).

```js
const addTax = (total, rate) => total * (1 + rate);   // pure: predictable, testable
```

Pure functions are trivial to reason about and test — no setup, no surprises. Keep your logic pure and
push the side effects (DOM writes, network) to the edges.

## Immutability — copy, don't mutate

Changing an object/array that someone else holds causes action-at-a-distance bugs. Make a **new** one
instead, with spread or `map`:

```js
const user = { name: 'Ada', role: 'dev' };
const promoted = { ...user, role: 'lead' };   // user is untouched

const items = ['a', 'b'];
const more = [...items, 'c'];                  // items is untouched
```

<Callout type="do">Default to pure functions and immutable updates. Predictable inputs/outputs make code easier to follow, safer to change, and ready to unit-test.</Callout>

title: Async / await
Fetching Data
DOM Toolkit
Observers
JSON: parse & stringify
text:
JSON is **text**; your code works with **objects and arrays**. Two functions bridge the two. ## `JSON.parse` — text → value Turn a JSON string into a real object/array you can use: ```js const text = '{"name":"Ada","langs":["JS","Java"]}'; const obj = JSON.parse(text); obj.name; // "Ada" obj.langs[0]; // "JS" ``` You'll use it for data from `localStorage`, a `data-*` attribute, or any string source. (After `fetch`, `res.json()` already parses for you — see lesson 10.) ## `JSON.stringify` — value → text Turn an object/array back into a string — to save it, send it in a request body, or log it: ```js JSON.stringify(obj); // '{"name":"Ada","langs":["JS","Java"]}' JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2); // pretty-printed with 2-space indent ``` ## Try it Open the console 👇

`} js={`const text = '{"name":"Ada","langs":["JS","Java"]}'; const obj = JSON.parse(text); // text -> object console.log(obj.name, obj.langs[0]); // Ada JS const back = JSON.stringify(obj); // object -> text console.log(back); console.log(JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2)); // pretty-printed`} /> `JSON.parse` **throws** on invalid JSON — always guard it with `try/catch` (lesson 14) when the input isn't under your control.

Passing Parameters & DOM References

Assignment: Build a Team Directory
text:
Build one component that ties the whole track together: a **Team Directory** that loads people from a mock API, shapes the data, renders cards, and filters them live — all in the functional style. Build it in `ui.frontend`. Budget **~2 hours**. This combines the lessons — it is **not** a copy of any one demo. Data comes from the free **JSONPlaceholder** API: `https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users`. The markup you enhance: ```html

    ``` ## Build it - [ ] **Structure** — a functional component initialised on the root; keep its state in closures, scoped to the root - [ ] **Load** — fetch the people from the API, with a loading state and an error state - [ ] **Shape** — turn the raw response into just what a card needs, as a pure step - [ ] **Render** — build the cards from the shaped data and put them on the page - [ ] **Filter** — as the user types, show only the matching cards and keep a running count How you split the functions and name things is up to you — aim for clean, readable modules. Done when it loads from the API (with loading + error states), renders the cards, and filters live with a running count. Compare with the [answer](assignment-answer).

title: Assignment — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](assignment) first. It pulls together the track: functional structure, fetch with states, a pure transform, fragment rendering, and live filtering.</Callout>

```js
// directory.clientlibs.js
(() => {
  'use strict';

  const SELECTORS = {
    root: '.directory',
    search: '.directory__search',
    status: '.directory__status',
    list: '.directory__list'
  };

  // pure: raw API users -> the shape we render, sorted, no mutation
  const shape = (users) =>
    users
      .map((u) => ({ name: u.name, email: u.email, company: u.company.name }))
      .sort((a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name));

  const renderList = (listEl, people) => {
    const frag = document.createDocumentFragment();
    people.forEach((person) => {
      const li = document.createElement('li');
      li.className = 'directory__item';
      li.dataset.name = person.name.toLowerCase();
      li.textContent = person.name + ' — ' + person.company;
      frag.appendChild(li);
    });
    listEl.replaceChildren(frag);
  };

  const init = async (root) => {
    const search = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.search);
    const status = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.status);
    const list = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.list);
    if (!search || !status || !list) return;

    let people = [];

    // fetch + states
    status.textContent = 'Loading…';
    try {
      const res = await fetch(root.dataset.endpoint);
      if (!res.ok) throw new Error('HTTP ' + res.status);
      people = shape(await res.json());
    } catch (err) {
      status.textContent = 'Could not load the team: ' + err.message;
      return;
    }

    renderList(list, people);

    const update = () => {
      const q = search.value.trim().toLowerCase();
      const items = list.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.list + ' > li');
      let shown = 0;
      items.forEach((li) => {
        const match = li.dataset.name.includes(q);
        li.hidden = !match;
        if (match) shown += 1;
      });
      status.textContent = 'showing ' + shown + ' of ' + people.length;
    };

    search.addEventListener('input', update);
    update();
  };

  const initAll = () => document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);

  document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', initAll);
})();
```

## Why it's shaped this way

- **Functional structure** (lessons 03, 15): an IIFE, `SELECTORS`, `init(root)`/`initAll`, config read from `root.dataset.endpoint` — state (`people`) lives in the closure.
- **Fetch with states** (lessons 10, 14): loading first, `res.ok` checked, errors caught and shown — it never throws uncaught.
- **Pure transform** (lessons 06, 08): `shape` maps + sorts a **copy**; given the same response it always returns the same list, so it's trivial to reason about.
- **Fragment render** (lesson 11): one DOM write, `textContent` (no injection), `data-name` for filtering.
- **Live filter** (lessons 05, 06): one `input` listener, toggles `hidden`, keeps a running count.

<Callout type="do">Notice the side effects (fetch, DOM writes) sit at the edges of `init`, while `shape` stays pure — that split is what makes the logic easy to follow and change.</Callout>

text:
Before the AEM-specific lessons, prove the **language** fundamentals on a plain HTML page — **no build, no AEM, no component loader**. Just a `.html` file (or the playground below) and the browser. This pulls together lessons **01–05**: variables & types, the **functional component** pattern (closures, `init`/`initAll`), **modern JS**, and **events**. Everything here is pure browser JavaScript, written the **functional** way — the style you'll use most in the project. Next lessons wrap these same ideas in the AEM component pattern. ## The task — a Tabs widget, built functionally You're given this markup. Make the tabs work: clicking a tab shows its panel and hides the others — using an `init`/`initAll` pattern. ```html

Panel A

`} css={`.tabs__tab { padding: .4rem .8rem; border: 0; background: #eee; cursor: pointer; } .tabs__tab[aria-selected="true"] { background: #6b2fb3; color: #fff; } .tabs__panel { padding: 1rem; border: 1px solid #ddd; margin-top: .5rem; }`} js={`const SELECTORS = { root: '.tabs', list: '.tabs__list', tab: '.tabs__tab', panel: '.tabs__panel' }; const init = (root) => { const tabs = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.tab); const panels = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.panel); const select = (name) => { // TODO: show the matching panel, hide the rest, update aria-selected }; // TODO: one delegated click listener on the list → call select(...) }; const initAll = () => document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init); initAll();`} /> Done when clicking any tab swaps the panel, only one panel is visible at a time, `aria-selected` follows the active tab, and two `.tabs` on the page work independently. Compare with the [answer](checkpoint-assignment-answer). ## Stretch - [ ] Support **two** `.tabs` widgets on the page at once — `initAll()` already loops, so confirm each one works independently (that's the closure giving each its own state). - [ ] Guard against a missing panel with optional chaining so a typo in `data-tab` doesn't throw. - [ ] Wrap the whole thing in an **IIFE** so nothing leaks to the global scope.

title: Checkpoint — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) first. The point is the functional pattern: `init` per element, state in a **closure**, and clicks handled by **delegation** — all in plain browser JS.</Callout>

## The solution

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.tabs', list: '.tabs__list', tab: '.tabs__tab', panel: '.tabs__panel' };

const init = (root) => {
  const list   = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.list);
  const tabs   = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.tab);
  const panels = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.panel);
  if (!list) return;

  const select = (name) => {
    const panel = root.querySelector(`[data-panel="${name}"]`);
    if (!panel) return;
    panels.forEach(p => p.hidden = p !== panel);
    tabs.forEach(t => t.setAttribute('aria-selected', t.dataset.tab === name));
  };

  list.addEventListener('click', (event) => {
    const tab = event.target.closest(SELECTORS.tab);
    if (tab?.dataset.tab) select(tab.dataset.tab);
  });
};

const initAll = () => document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);

document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', initAll);
```

## Why it's shaped this way

- **A closure per widget:** each `init(root)` call captures its own `list`/`tabs`/`panels`, so two tab groups on a page never clash — the closure does the job a class instance did (lesson 03).
- **State in the closure:** `select` and the click handler close over the local `list`/`tabs`/`panels`, so each widget keeps its own state.
- **One delegated listener:** a single `click` on `.tabs__list`, not one per tab — read the target with `closest()` (lesson 05).
- **Modern JS:** NodeList `.forEach`, a template-literal selector, optional chaining (`tab?.dataset.tab`), and `setAttribute` coercing the boolean to `"true"`/`"false"` (lesson 04).

<Callout type="do">If your widget uses `init`/`initAll`, keeps state in the closure, and handles clicks with one delegated listener, you've nailed the functional pattern the project leans on — the AEM component lessons build straight on it.</Callout>

title: Practice Drills: Functional Components
Practice Drills — Answers
text:
<Callout type="note">Teacher reference — reachable only by URL, hidden from the menu. One correct solution per drill; learners should attempt the [drills](component-drills) first.</Callout>

## Level 1 — Toggle

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.toggle', btn: '.toggle__btn', panel: '.toggle__panel' };

const init = (root) => {
  const btn = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.btn);
  const panel = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.panel);
  if (!btn || !panel) return;

  btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
    const willOpen = panel.hidden;
    panel.hidden = !willOpen;
    btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(willOpen));
  });
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

## Level 2 — Star rating

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.rating', star: '.rating__star' };

const init = (root) => {
  const stars = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.star);

  const render = (value) => {
    root.dataset.value = value;
    stars.forEach((star) => {
      star.classList.toggle('rating__star--on', Number(star.dataset.value) <= value);
    });
  };

  stars.forEach((star) => {
    star.addEventListener('click', () => render(Number(star.dataset.value)));
  });

  render(Number(root.dataset.value) || 0);
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

## Level 3 — Accordion

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.accordion', header: '.accordion__header', item: '.accordion__item', panel: '.accordion__panel' };

const init = (root) => {
  const headers = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.header);

  headers.forEach((header) => {
    header.addEventListener('click', () => {
      const panel = header.closest(SELECTORS.item).querySelector(SELECTORS.panel);
      const willOpen = panel.hidden;
      panel.hidden = !willOpen;
      header.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(willOpen));
    });
  });
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

<Callout type="note">Single-open stretch: before opening one, close the others — `headers.forEach(h => { h.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false'); h.closest(SELECTORS.item).querySelector(SELECTORS.panel).hidden = true; });` then open the clicked one.</Callout>

## Level 4 — Live filter

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.filter', input: '.filter__input', item: '.filter__list li' };

const init = (root) => {
  const input = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.input);
  const items = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.item);
  if (!input) return;

  input.addEventListener('input', () => {
    const q = input.value.trim().toLowerCase();
    items.forEach((item) => {
      item.hidden = !item.textContent.toLowerCase().includes(q);
    });
  });
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

## Level 5 — Carousel

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.carousel', btn: '.carousel__btn', slide: '.carousel__slide' };

const init = (root) => {
  const slides = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.slide);
  const buttons = root.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.btn);
  if (!slides.length) return;

  let current = 0;

  const show = (index) => {
    current = (index + slides.length) % slides.length;   // wrap around
    slides.forEach((slide, i) => { slide.hidden = i !== current; });
  };

  buttons.forEach((btn) => {
    btn.addEventListener('click', () => show(current + Number(btn.dataset.dir)));
  });

  show(0);
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

title: JS Components & the Component Loader
text:
In Netcentric/AEM projects, JavaScript is initialised through the **Component Loader** — an open-source library that wires DOM elements to JS classes. You don't call your component yourself; you **register** it, mark up an element, and the loader creates an instance for you. ## What it does - Scans the DOM for elements that declare a component (via a `data-nc` attribute). - Creates a **class instance** for each match, passing the element + options. - Keeps watching the DOM and initialises **new** elements added later — essential for a WYSIWYG CMS like AEM, where authors add components live. ## What you need to wire up a component For the loader to pick up and run your component, **five things** must line up. Miss one and nothing happens (no error) — so it's worth knowing them all. 1. **A class that accepts `(element, options)`.** The loader calls `new YourClass(element, options)` for every match. Keep the constructor tiny: store them and call `init()`. 2. **Register the class** with `register({ MyComponent })`. The **name you register** (`MyComponent`) is the exact name the loader will look for in the HTML. 3. **Make sure the build includes it** — export it from the publish entry (`index.js`) so the bundler ships it. Code that's never imported never runs. 4. **Mark up the element** with `data-nc="MyComponent"`. The value must match the registered name **exactly** (it's case-sensitive). 5. **The loader runtime must run on the page** — `run()` + `observe()`, loaded via the publish JS bundle. Without it, nothing initialises at all. Optional, but common: 6. **Pass configuration** with `data-nc-params-MyComponent='{ … }'` (valid JSON). It arrives in the constructor as `options` — covered in the next lesson. ## Registering a component ```js import { register } from '@netcentric/component-loader'; class MyComponent { constructor(element, options) { this.element = element; // the DOM node with data-nc="MyComponent" this.options = options; // parsed from data-nc-params-… this.init(); } init() { // do something with this.element … } } register({ MyComponent }); ``` ## Wiring it from HTML The loader matches the registered name to elements carrying the `data-nc` attribute: ```html
…
``` On `DOMContentLoaded` the loader checks every registered component against the DOM; on a match, the class is instantiated. ## The lifecycle, end to end ```mermaid flowchart TD H["HTML: data-nc = MyComponent"] -->|loader scans DOM
and watches for new nodes| N["new MyComponent(element, options)"] N -->|constructor stores refs| I["init() — your behaviour starts"] ``` Keep the constructor tiny — store `element`/`options`, then call `init()`. Put real setup (queries, listeners) in `init()` so the flow is easy to read. ## Why isn't my component running? When a component silently does nothing (and there's **no error**), it's almost always one of the five requirements above: - the `data-nc` value **doesn't match** the registered name (typo or wrong case); - the class is **never imported/exported**, so the build left it out; - the **publish JS bundle isn't loaded** on the page, so the loader never runs; - the `data-nc-params-…` value is **not valid JSON** (single quotes, trailing comma) and fails to parse. A mismatch produces **no error** — the loader just finds nothing to do. When in doubt, walk the checklist below: it's nearly always a name mismatch or a missing bundle. ## Quick checklist - [ ] A class that takes `(element, options)` and does setup in `init()` - [ ] `register({ MyComponent })` - [ ] Exported from the publish entry so the build bundles it - [ ] `data-nc="MyComponent"` on the element — name matches **exactly** - [ ] Publish JS bundle (loader `run()` / `observe()`) loaded on the page - [ ] *(optional)* config via `data-nc-params-MyComponent='{ … }'` as valid JSON The loader is open source — you can read and contribute to it. Next we'll pass data in (parameters) and find inner elements (DOM references).

title: Practice: Fetch, async & arrays
Practice — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [practice](fetch-practice) first.</Callout>

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.todos', status: '.todos__status', list: '.todos__list' };

const init = async (root) => {
  const status = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.status);
  const list = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.list);

  status.textContent = 'Loading…';

  let todos;
  try {
    const res = await fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos');
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('HTTP ' + res.status);
    todos = (await res.json()).slice(0, 10);
  } catch (err) {
    status.textContent = 'Failed: ' + err.message;
    return;
  }

  const done = todos.filter((t) => t.completed).length;   // array methods

  const frag = document.createDocumentFragment();
  todos.forEach((t) => {
    const li = document.createElement('li');
    li.textContent = (t.completed ? '✓ ' : '○ ') + t.title;
    frag.appendChild(li);
  });
  list.replaceChildren(frag);                              // one DOM write

  status.textContent = done + ' of ' + todos.length + ' done';
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

<Callout type="do">Note the shape: an `async init`, loading + error states around the `fetch`, array methods for the count, and a single fragment write — the same moves the [Team Directory](assignment) capstone uses at full size.</Callout>

title: Final Project: Blog Explorer
text:
Build a small app — a **Blog Explorer** — that puts most of the track to work: fetch real data, transform it, render it, filter it, and open details. Build it in the **`kbc-fe`** project (the Node/Vite front-end your trainer provides), with **ES modules**, **semantic HTML**, and **SASS**. Data comes from [JSONPlaceholder](https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/): `/posts`, `/users`, `/posts/{id}/comments`. ## How it should look ```text ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Blog Explorer [ search posts… ] │ ← header + SearchBar ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ┌────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ │ │ │ Post title │ │ Post title │ │ Post title │ │ ← PostList: grid of cards │ │ by Author │ │ by Author │ │ by Author │ │ (
), fade in │ │ excerpt…… │ │ excerpt…… │ │ excerpt…… │ │ on scroll │ └────────────┘ └────────────┘ └────────────┘ │ │ … more cards reveal as you scroll … │ └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ Click a card → PostDialog () ┌──────────────────────────────────┐ │ Post title ✕ │ │ by Author │ │ body text…… │ │ ─── Comments ─── │ │ • Name — comment text… │ │ • Name — comment text… │ └──────────────────────────────────┘ ``` Responsive: 1 column on mobile, 2–3 as it widens (SASS + a breakpoint mixin). ## The three components Each is a **factory function** that sets up its element. They're wired together in `main.js` — mostly by **callbacks** and **method calls**, with **one** place using a custom event. 1. **SearchBar** — `createSearchBar(root, { onSearch })`. A `
` with a search input; on input (**debounced**) it calls `onSearch(query)`. 2. **PostList** — `createPostList(root)` returns **`{ render(posts), filter(query) }`**. It renders cards into a grid and **fades them in** with an IntersectionObserver. When a card is clicked it dispatches a single **`post:open`** `CustomEvent` (bubbling, the post in `detail`). 3. **PostDialog** — `createPostDialog(root)` **listens for `post:open`**, then fetches that post's comments and shows the title, body, and comments in a ``, with loading and error states. The one custom event decouples the cards from the dialog — many cards, a single listener. Everything else is plain wiring in `main.js`: ```js createPostDialog(dialogRoot); // listens for 'post:open' const list = createPostList(listRoot); // cards dispatch 'post:open' createSearchBar(searchRoot, { onSearch: (q) => list.filter(q) }); const posts = await getPosts(); const users = await getUsers(); list.render(joinAuthors(posts, users)); ``` ## Suggested module layout ```text src/ ├── api.js # getPosts(), getUsers(), getComments(id) — fetch + res.json() ├── utils.js # debounce · el/excerpt DOM helpers · joinAuthors ├── components/ │ ├── search-bar.js # createSearchBar(root, { onSearch }) │ ├── post-list.js # createPostList(root) -> { render, filter }; cards dispatch 'post:open' │ └── post-dialog.js # createPostDialog(root); listens for 'post:open' ├── styles/ │ ├── _tokens.scss # colors, spacing, breakpoints │ ├── _post-card.scss # BEM component styles │ └── main.scss # @use the partials └── main.js # create + wire the components ``` ## Requirements - [ ] **Modules** — split the API, helpers, and each component into their own files; `main.js` wires them - [ ] **Load** — fetch the posts and the authors, with loading + error states - [ ] **Transform** — shape the data with pure helpers (join authors, build excerpts), no mutation - [ ] **Render** — build the cards from the shaped data and put them on the page - [ ] **Compose** — factories return APIs; coordinate with callbacks and method calls - [ ] **One custom event** — a card click dispatches `post:open` and the dialog listens; nothing else uses events - [ ] **Lazy-reveal** the cards and **debounce** the search - [ ] **Semantic HTML** — `
`, `
`, `
`, ``, `` - [ ] **SASS** — tokens + BEM component partials and a breakpoint mixin How you split and name the functions is up to you — aim for clean, readable modules. ## Acceptance Done when: posts load (each card showing its author), the search filters live, cards fade in on scroll, clicking a card opens a dialog with that post's comments, and every fetch shows loading/handles errors — all in clean functional modules, semantic markup, and SASS. Keep side effects (fetch, DOM writes) inside the components; keep the transforms in pure helpers. That split is what makes the modules easy to read, reuse, and change. ## Expected result What you're aiming for — **the grid** (filter bar + cards):

Blog Explorer

title: Assignment: Posts Feed (Load more)
text:
Build a **Posts Feed** that loads posts and reveals them **a page at a time** behind a "Load more" button — functional style, plain browser JS. Build it in `ui.frontend`. Budget **~1 hour**. Data from [JSONPlaceholder](https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/) `/posts`. The markup you enhance: ```html

    ``` ## Build it - [ ] **Structure** — a functional component on the root; keep the page state in a closure - [ ] **Load** — fetch the posts once, with a loading state and an error state - [ ] **Page** — show the first batch; on "Load more", **add** the next batch (don't re-render what's already there) - [ ] **Stop** — hide the button when there's nothing left, and keep a running count How you split and name the functions is up to you. Done when the first batch loads, each click appends the next, the count keeps up, the button disappears at the end, and a failed request shows a message. Compare with the [answer](posts-feed-answer).

title: Posts Feed — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](posts-feed) first.</Callout>

```js
const SELECTORS = { root: '.feed', list: '.feed__list', more: '.feed__more', status: '.feed__status' };
const PAGE_SIZE = 10;

const init = async (root) => {
  const list = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.list);
  const moreBtn = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.more);
  const status = root.querySelector(SELECTORS.status);

  let posts = [];
  let shown = 0;                 // page state — lives in the closure

  status.textContent = 'Loading…';
  moreBtn.hidden = true;
  try {
    const res = await fetch('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts');
    if (!res.ok) throw new Error('HTTP ' + res.status);
    posts = await res.json();
  } catch (err) {
    status.textContent = 'Failed: ' + err.message;
    return;
  }

  const renderNextPage = () => {
    const next = posts.slice(shown, shown + PAGE_SIZE);
    const frag = document.createDocumentFragment();
    next.forEach((post) => {
      const li = document.createElement('li');
      li.className = 'feed__item';
      li.textContent = post.title;
      frag.appendChild(li);
    });
    list.appendChild(frag);                    // append — keep what's already there
    shown += next.length;
    status.textContent = 'showing ' + shown + ' of ' + posts.length;
    moreBtn.hidden = shown >= posts.length;    // nothing left → hide the button
  };

  moreBtn.addEventListener('click', renderNextPage);
  renderNextPage();                            // first page
};

document.querySelectorAll(SELECTORS.root).forEach(init);
```

## Why it's shaped this way

- **State in the closure** (lesson 07): `posts` and `shown` are private to each `init(root)` — two feeds on a page page independently.
- **Fetch once** (lessons 10, 14): one request with loading + error states; paging is pure array work afterwards.
- **`slice` + append** (lessons 06, 11): each page is `posts.slice(shown, shown + PAGE_SIZE)`, built in a fragment and **appended** so earlier items stay.
- **Self-limiting** (lesson 05): the button hides itself once `shown` reaches the end.

title: What is a Preprocessor
text:
We don't write CSS to deliver CSS — we write **SCSS** and let a build step compile it. A **preprocessor** gives us a more comfortable syntax with variables, nesting, functions and mixins, then outputs plain CSS the browser understands. ## Why bother? Plain CSS has real limits at scale: - lots of **repetition** - **no reusability** of logic - **no real logic** (conditionals, loops) - limited **flexibility** Modern front-end deals with complexity through architecture (BEM, ITCSS, OOCSS), design systems — and preprocessors are one of the tools that make that practical. ## Where it fits in the build ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SCSS you write"] -->|preprocess / compile| B["CSS"] B -->|postprocess| C["Optimised CSS
autoprefixed + minified"] C --> D["Shipped to the browser"] ``` In this project that's `nc-fe-build`: it compiles `*.scss` into clientlib CSS, then post-processes (autoprefixing, etc.). ## SCSS vs Sass syntax There are two syntaxes. **SCSS** is a superset of CSS (curly braces and semicolons — every valid CSS file is valid SCSS), and it's what we use: ```scss $brand: #6b2fb3; .button { background: $brand; &:hover { filter: brightness(1.1); } } ``` Because SCSS is a superset of CSS, you can paste existing CSS straight in and improve it incrementally — no rewrite needed. ## Trying it quickly The [Sass playground](https://sass-lang.com/playground/) compiles SCSS → CSS live in the browser — handy for experimenting with the features in the next lessons. Preprocessor power is a means, not an end. The output is still CSS — keep it lean, and don't let nesting or clever functions hide what the final CSS actually does.

title: Values & Operators
Nesting & the & selector
Functions & Mixins
Styling an AEM Component
text:
In AEM, a component's CSS is delivered as a **client library** (clientlib). In this project you author **SCSS** in `ui.frontend`, the build compiles it into a clientlib under a **category**, and the component loads that category from its HTL. ## The end-to-end flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S["SCSS
ui.frontend/.../teaser.clientlibs.scss"] --> B["nc-fe-build
compile + minify"] B --> C["clientlib
category: academy.publish.components.teaser"] C --> H["teaser.html
data-sly-call clientlib.all"] H --> P["styled component on the page"] ``` ## Where the SCSS lives ```text ui.frontend/src/main/frontend/academy/publish/components/teaser/ └── teaser.clientlibs.scss ``` The build derives the **category from the folder path**: `academy/publish/components/teaser` → `academy.publish.components.teaser`. ## Write the styles (the project pattern) The project uses a `$module-name` variable + BEM (enforced by stylelint — next lesson): ```scss @use "variables/colors" as *; $module-name: "teaser"; .#{$module-name} { &__title { font-size: 2rem; color: var(--brand); } &__cta { text-decoration: underline; } &--muted { opacity: .7; } } ``` ## Load it from the component's HTL Pull the category in with the Granite `clientlib` template: ```html

${teaser.title}

``` - `clientlib.all` injects CSS **and** JS; `clientlib.css` / `clientlib.js` load just one. - The `categories` string must match the generated category **exactly**. One SCSS folder per component, named to match the component, producing one predictable category. That keeps the clientlib graph obvious as the project grows. This mirrors the Component Creation track's "Adding the Clientlibs" lesson — here we focus on the **SCSS authoring** side; there on the component wiring.

title: Project Style Architecture
BEM in AEM Components
text:
This project styles components with **BEM** (Block · Element · Modifier), enforced by stylelint. BEM keeps selectors flat (single classes), so styles are easy to override on purpose and hard to break by accident — which matters when authors compose many components on a page. ## The convention ```text .teaser Block — the component root .teaser__title Element — a part of the block .teaser--featured Modifier — a variant of the block .teaser__title--big Element modifier ``` ## The `$module-name` pattern Components define the block name once and build selectors with `&`: ```scss $module-name: "teaser"; .#{$module-name} { display: grid; &__title { font-size: 2rem; } // .teaser__title &__cta { text-decoration: underline; } // .teaser__cta &--featured { // .teaser--featured .#{$module-name}__title { color: var(--brand); } } } ``` Rename the component once (`$module-name`) and every selector follows. ## Match the markup The HTL carries the same classes, so markup and styles line up one-to-one: ```html

${teaser.title}

Read more
``` ## Keep specificity flat Deeply nested selectors (`.teaser .body .title a span`) — high specificity, hard to override, brittle when an author reorders components. One class per element. Nest at most ~2 levels, and only to scope a modifier (`.teaser--featured .teaser__title`). The output selector should stay a single class wherever possible. ## Stylelint enforces it The project's stylelint config requires kebab-case BEM class names and flags violations in the build: ```js // stylelint.config.js (project) 'selector-class-pattern': '^[a-z]([a-z0-9-]+)?(__([a-z0-9-]+))?(--([a-z0-9-]+))?$' ``` A class that doesn't match the BEM pattern fails lint. Stick to `block__element--modifier`, all lowercase, kebab-case — no camelCase, no IDs for styling. ## It powers the Style System AEM's **Style System** lets authors pick visual variants from the component toolbar — and under the hood, those variants are just **BEM modifier classes**. The flow: 1. You define the modifier in SCSS **once** — `.teaser--featured { … }`. 2. The template policy gets a `cq:styleGroups` entry for that variant: a label (*"Featured"*) mapped to the class `teaser--featured`. 3. In the page, the author picks **Featured** from the component's toolbar. 4. AEM adds `teaser--featured` to the component's element — and the SCSS you already wrote styles it. A new author-selectable variant is usually **no new code**: one more BEM modifier in your SCSS, plus one entry in the policy. (Policies & the Style System are covered in the Components track.)

title: CSS Best Practices for AEM
text:
Pages are composed from many independently-authored components. These practices keep one
component's CSS from breaking another's — and keep pages fast.

## Scope styles to the component

<Callout type="dont">Bare element selectors leak across the whole page: `h2 { color: red; }`, `ul { margin: 0; }`, `a { … }`. Any other component's `h2`/`ul`/`a` is now affected.</Callout>
<Callout type="do">Namespace everything under the component's block class: `.teaser h2` or, better, `.teaser__title`. A component should only ever style **its own** subtree.</Callout>

## Keep specificity low and flat

- One class per element (BEM). Avoid IDs and long descendant chains.
- Never reach for `!important` to win a fight — fix the selector or use a modifier class. (If you
  must override a library, prefer **cascade layers** — see the CSS track.)

## Troubleshooting: my SCSS change isn't in the CSS

SCSS is a **build input** — AEM never serves your `.scss` directly. The front-end build compiles it
into the clientlib's CSS, and the *compiled* file is what loads. So when an edit "doesn't show up", the
new CSS almost always wasn't **produced** or wasn't **deployed**. Check these, most common first:

1. **You didn't rebuild + redeploy.** Editing `.scss` does nothing until the FE build runs. Rebuild and install the front-end module: `mvn clean install -PautoInstallPackage -pl ui.frontend`. Until then AEM keeps serving the previously compiled CSS.
2. **The build failed, so no new file was written.** A SCSS compile error — or a **stylelint** error — aborts the bundle, and AEM keeps the last good CSS. Read the build log, fix the error, rebuild. (A green "BUILD SUCCESS" line is not enough — scroll up for stylelint warnings/errors.)
3. **The partial isn't imported.** A new `_card.scss` only compiles if an entry file `@use`s / `@imports` it. An orphan partial is silently ignored — no error, no output.
4. **You edited the compiled output, not the source.** Generated CSS (under `dist` / the clientlib output) is **overwritten on every build**. Edit the `.scss` source, never the built `.css`.
5. **Something is caching the old file.** Hard-refresh the browser (it caches the hashed clientlib URL); rebuild AEM's clientlibs via `/libs/granite/ui/content/dumplibs.rebuild.html`; and on **publish**, the **dispatcher** may serve a cached copy — flush it.
6. **The clientlib category isn't on the page.** The CSS compiled fine, but that category isn't included (HTL clientlib include / page policy), so you never see it. Confirm the `<link>` is actually in the page source.

<Callout type="do">The golden rule: **edit the `.scss`, rebuild, redeploy, hard-refresh.** If the change still isn't there, the build almost certainly **failed** (check the log) or you're editing the **compiled output** instead of the source.</Callout>

## Performance (ties into the Web Performance track)

- Ship only what a page needs — per-component clientlibs beat one giant global stylesheet.
- CSS is **render-blocking**: keep it lean, minified (the build does this), and consider
  **critical CSS** for above-the-fold content.
- Style media with `width`/`height` or `aspect-ratio` to avoid layout shift (CLS).
- Prefer animating `transform`/`opacity` (compositor-friendly) over layout properties.

## Theming & variants

- Expose brand/spacing as **CSS custom properties** so themes and the **Style System** can override
  them live, without recompiling.
- Author-selectable variants = BEM **modifier** classes toggled by `cq:styleGroups` (Components
  track) — no per-variant code.

## Responsive

- Mobile-first: base styles for small screens, layer up with `min-width` media queries using the
  **shared breakpoints** (`common/styles`).
- Lean on intrinsic sizing (Flexbox/Grid wrapping, `min()`/`max()`/`clamp()`) so a component adapts
  to the space it's given with as few media queries as possible.

## Quick checklist

<Callout type="do">Before you commit a component's SCSS, check: scoped to the block class · BEM, low specificity · no `!important` · uses shared tokens, not hard-coded values · author-only styles behind `wcmmode` · media sized to avoid CLS · passes stylelint.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">Together with the Components track (clientlibs, policies, Style System) and the Web Performance track (critical CSS, fonts, images), this is the full picture of styling AEM components well.</Callout>

title: Assignment: Architect & Apply SCSS
text:
Pick up the SCSS you wrote in the [mid-module checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) and turn it into a
**maintainable architecture** — partials, `@use`/`@forward`, token maps — then apply it the way you
would on a real AEM component. Applies lessons **05–09**. Budget **~2 hours**. Build it in your
project's `ui.frontend` SCSS.

<Callout type="note">Extend the same SCSS — don't restart. If you skipped the checkpoint, do [Part 1](checkpoint-assignment) first (variables, nesting, functions, mixins); everything here organizes and applies those.</Callout>

## Part 1 — Architecture (Intermediate)

- [ ] Split into **partials**: `_variables.scss`, `_mixins.scss`, component partials
- [ ] Wire them with **`@use` / `@forward`** (no `@import`) — namespaced where it helps
- [ ] Put your tokens in a **map** (e.g. `$spacing: (sm: .5rem, md: 1rem, lg: 2rem)`) and read with `map.get`
- [ ] Generate a spacing/utility set with **`@each`**
- [ ] A **`@mixin`** breakpoint helper driven by your breakpoints map

**Acceptance:** one entry point (`_index.scss`) `@forward`s the shared layer; components `@use` tokens instead of redefining them; no `@import`; changing a token ripples everywhere.

## Part 2 — Apply it to an AEM component (Advanced)

- [ ] Author **one component's styles as its own partial** (`hello.clientlibs.scss`-style), `@use`-ing the shared tokens/mixins — never redefining them
- [ ] Use **BEM-in-AEM** naming so the compiled selectors are flat, single-class, and namespaced to the component
- [ ] Sanity-check the **compiled output** is lean — no deep selector chains, no duplicated declarations

**Acceptance:** the component partial reuses the shared layer (no copy-pasted tokens); selectors stay flat BEM.

## Stretch goals

- A typography mixin (`font-size` + `line-height` from a scale).
- Organise files ITCSS-style (settings → tools → components).
- Add a `map`-driven theme and switch it with `@each`.

<Callout type="do">Done when: a single change (a token, a mixin) ripples through the whole stylesheet, the file structure reads clearly, the component partial reuses the shared layer, and the compiled CSS is no bigger than your hand-written version.</Callout>

title: Assignment — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](assignment) first. The goal is one source of truth: tokens in a map, an `_index.scss` that `@forward`s the shared layer, and components that `@use` it rather than redefine it.</Callout>

## File structure

```text
common/styles/
├── _tokens.scss     ← variables + maps
├── _mixins.scss     ← button(), bp() …
└── index.scss       ← @forward barrel (the entry point)
publish/components/card/
└── card.clientlibs.scss
```

## The shared layer

```scss
// _tokens.scss
$brand: #6b2fb3;
$spacing: (sm: .5rem, md: 1rem, lg: 2rem);
$breakpoints: (md: 48rem, lg: 64rem);
```

```scss
// _mixins.scss
@use "sass:map";
@use "tokens" as *;

@mixin bp($name) {
  @media (min-width: map.get($breakpoints, $name)) { @content; }
}

@mixin button($bg, $fg: #fff) {
  padding: map.get($spacing, sm) map.get($spacing, md);
  border: 0; border-radius: 6px;
  background: $bg; color: $fg; cursor: pointer;
}
```

```scss
// index.scss — the entry point: @forward passes the partials along
@forward "tokens";
@forward "mixins";
```

## Generate utilities with `@each`

```scss
@use "tokens" as *;
@each $name, $val in $spacing {
  .p-#{$name} { padding: $val; }     // .p-sm / .p-md / .p-lg
}
```

## Apply it on a component (flat BEM, author-guarded)

```scss
// card.clientlibs.scss
@use "../../../common/styles" as *;   // the _index entry point — tokens + mixins in one

.card {
  padding: 1rem;
  @include bp(md) { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 120px 1fr; }
}
.card__cta { @include button($brand); }

// edit-only styling, behind author mode
.cq-Editable-dom .card { outline: 1px dashed $brand; }
```

<Callout type="do">Change a token or a mixin once and every consumer updates; the compiled CSS stays flat single-class BEM, and author-only rules never reach publish.</Callout>

title: Checkpoint: SCSS Language Basics
Checkpoint — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">Attempt the [checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) first. This covers the SCSS language basics; the [final assignment](assignment) splits it into a maintainable architecture.</Callout>

```scss
@use "sass:math";

// variables — one source of truth
$brand: #6b2fb3;
$radius: 6px;
$space: 1rem;

// @function — returns a value, emits no CSS
@function rem($px, $base: 16px) {
  @return math.div($px, $base) * 1rem;
}

// @mixin — emits CSS, with default args and an @if decision
@mixin button($bg, $fg: #fff) {
  display: inline-flex;
  padding: math.div($space, 2) $space;
  border: 0;
  border-radius: $radius;
  background: $bg;
  color: $fg;
  cursor: pointer;

  @if $bg == transparent {
    border: 1px solid $brand;   // the ghost variant needs an outline
  }

  &:hover { filter: brightness(1.08); }
}

// nesting + & for BEM, the mixin reused for two variants
.btn {
  font-size: rem(16px);
  @include button($brand);

  &--ghost { @include button(transparent, $brand); }
}
```

<Callout type="do">Change `$brand` or the `button` mixin and every consumer updates. Nesting stays ≤ 2 levels, so the compiled output is flat, single-class BEM — no copy-pasted blocks.</Callout>

title: Assignment: Breakpoint Mixin & Responsive Component
text:
The shared layer already has breakpoint **tokens** (`$tablet`, `$desktop`, `$large-desktop`) — but no
clean way to *use* them, so a component would hand-write `@media (min-width: 600px)` everywhere. In
this assignment you build a reusable, map-driven **breakpoint mixin**, `@forward` it from the shared
layer, and use it to make a real component **responsive** and **token-driven**. Build it in your
project's `ui.frontend` SCSS. Budget **~1.5 hours**.

<Callout type="note">This builds on the [architecture assignment](assignment): you already have `common/styles` with tokens forwarded from `variables.scss`. Here you add the **mixin layer** on top and apply it to an existing component.</Callout>

## Part 1 — Build the breakpoint mixin

- [ ] Add a `mixins.scss` partial in `common/styles`
- [ ] Build a **`$breakpoints` map** from the existing tokens (`tablet`, `desktop`, `large`)
- [ ] Write a **`@mixin bp($name)`** that uses `@use "sass:map"`, looks the name up with `map.get`, and wraps **`@content`** in a `@media (min-width: …)` query

<Reveal title="Starter skeleton">

```scss
// common/styles/mixins.scss
@use "sass:map";
@use "variables/breakpoints" as *;

$breakpoints: (
  tablet:  $tablet,
  // …add desktop and large
);

@mixin bp($name) {
  // 1. look up the min-width for $name
  // 2. @media (min-width: …) { @content; }
}
```

</Reveal>

**Acceptance:** `@include bp(tablet) { … }` compiles to a `@media (min-width: 600px)` block containing
your styles, and the breakpoint values come from the **tokens**, not hard-coded numbers.

## Part 2 — Expose it through the barrel

- [ ] `@forward "mixins";` from `variables.scss` so any component already doing `@use "variables" as *` gets `bp` automatically

**Acceptance:** a component `@use`s **one** module (`variables`) and can call `bp(...)` — no extra import, no relative path to the mixin file.

## Part 3 — Make the `account-compare` component responsive

Style `academy/publish/components/account-compare/account-compare.clientlibs.scss`. The component
renders **3 cards** side by side (e.g. comparing account tiers) — but 3 columns don't fit a phone, so
it needs to adapt.

- [ ] Use a **mobile-first** layout: the 3 cards **stacked** in one column
- [ ] Scale the grid up with the mixin — **`@include bp(tablet)`** → 2 columns, **`@include bp(desktop)`** → all **3 side by side**
- [ ] Style the cards with **tokens** (colors, borders) — no hard-coded hex
- [ ] Keep it **flat BEM** (`.account-compare__card`, `.account-compare__title`), nesting ≤ 2 levels

**Acceptance:** the component reuses the shared mixin + tokens; the cards stack on mobile and become 3
columns on desktop; the compiled CSS has a mobile-first base plus `min-width` media blocks, with no
duplicated declarations.

## Verify

- [ ] Rebuild the front end: `mvn clean install -PautoInstallPackage -pl ui.frontend`
- [ ] Author the component, resize the browser, and confirm the cards go 1 → 2 → 3 columns at tablet/desktop
- [ ] Open the compiled `account-compare` CSS and confirm the `@media (min-width: 600px / 992px)` blocks are there

## Stretch goals

- A `respond-between($min, $max)` mixin for "only this range".
- Guard `bp()` with **`@error`** when the name isn't in the map (so a typo fails the build loudly).
- A **typography mixin** that applies `font-size` + `line-height` from the shared font map.

<Callout type="do">Done when one mixin replaces every hand-written media query, the cards go 1 → 2 → 3 columns from a mobile-first base, all colors come from tokens, and changing a breakpoint token shifts every component that uses `bp()` at once.</Callout>

title: Responsive Assignment — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](responsive-assignment) first. The goal is a single map-driven `bp()` mixin, forwarded through the shared barrel, that replaces every hand-written media query.</Callout>

## 1 — The breakpoint mixin

```scss
// common/styles/mixins.scss
@use "sass:map";
@use "variables/breakpoints" as *;   // resolves via .febuild.js includePaths (common/styles)

$breakpoints: (
  tablet:  $tablet,        // 600px
  desktop: $desktop,       // 992px
  large:   $large-desktop, // 1200px
);

@mixin bp($name) {
  $min: map.get($breakpoints, $name);

  @if not $min {
    @error "Unknown breakpoint `#{$name}` — use one of: #{map.keys($breakpoints)}.";
  }

  @media (min-width: $min) {
    @content;
  }
}
```

The `@content` block is where the calling rule's styles are dropped, so `@include bp(tablet) { … }`
emits `@media (min-width: 600px) { … }`. The `@error` guard turns a typo (`bp(tablet)`) into a build
failure instead of silently-missing styles.

## 2 — Forward it through the barrel

```scss
// common/styles/variables.scss  (add this line)
@forward "mixins";
```

Now any component already doing `@use "variables" as *` gets `bp` — no separate import.

## 3 — The responsive `account-compare`

Three cards: **stacked** on mobile, **2 columns** at tablet, **3 side by side** at desktop — driven
entirely by the `bp()` mixin.

```scss
// academy/publish/components/account-compare/account-compare.clientlibs.scss
@charset "UTF-8";
@use "variables" as *;

$module-name: "account-compare";

.#{$module-name} {
  display: grid;
  gap: 1rem;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr;                       // mobile-first: stacked

  @include bp(tablet)  { grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr); }
  @include bp(desktop) { grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); }  // all 3 side by side

  &__card {
    padding: 1.25rem;
    border: 1px solid $c-grey-300;
    border-radius: 8px;
    background: $c-white;
  }

  &__title {
    color: $c-purple-700;
    font-size: 20px;                                // mobile
  }
  @include bp(desktop) {
    &__title { font-size: 24px; }
  }
}
```

The single grid declaration does the heavy lifting: one column by default, then `bp()` widens it to 2
and 3 columns. No per-card width math, no floats.

## Why this is the shape we want

- **One source of truth:** breakpoints live in the map; change `$tablet` and every `bp(tablet)` block moves with it.
- **Mobile-first:** the base is the stacked single column; `min-width` layers add columns only as the screen grows — fewer overrides, leaner CSS.
- **Reuse via `@forward`:** the component imports one module and calls `bp()` — the same `@use`/`@forward` split from [lesson 06](06-project-style-architecture).

<Callout type="do">Compiled output should be the mobile-first 1-column base followed by `@media (min-width: 600px)` / `(min-width: 992px)` blocks that widen the grid — no repeated declarations and no hand-typed pixel breakpoints anywhere in the component.</Callout>

title: What are Sling Jobs?
text:
A **Sling Job** is a unit of work you hand to the platform to run **asynchronously**, in the background, with a **guarantee** it will be processed — even across restarts. ## The model: producer → queue → consumer ```mermaid flowchart LR P["Producer
JobManager.addJob(topic, props)"] --> Q[("Job Queue
(persisted)")] Q --> C["JobConsumer
registered for the topic"] C -->|OK| Done([Removed from queue]) C -->|FAILED| Q C -->|CANCEL| Dead([Cancelled]) ``` A producer adds a job to a **topic**; the platform persists it in a **queue** and hands it to a registered **consumer**. If processing fails, the job is **retried**; nothing is silently lost. ## Why not just a thread or a scheduler? | | New `Thread` | Scheduler | **Sling Job** | |---|---|---|---| | Survives restart | ✗ | ✗ (just re-fires on schedule) | ✓ persisted + retried | | Guaranteed once | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (at-least-once) | | Distributed (cluster) | ✗ | runs per instance | ✓ processed once in the cluster | | Best for | nothing in AEM | *time-based* recurring tasks | *event-driven* background work | - **Scheduler** answers *"every night at 2am"* — recurring, time-based. - **Sling Jobs** answer *"do this reliably, soon, off the request thread"* — e.g. send an email after form submit, re-index something, call a slow API. Use a Sling Job to move slow or unreliable work **off the request/workflow thread** while guaranteeing it runs. Use the **Scheduler** for purely time-triggered recurring tasks. Workflows can **offload** heavy steps to a Sling Job (you saw this in *Workflows → Best Practices*) so the workflow thread isn't held by long I/O.

title: The Sling Job API
Assignment: A Background Job
text:
Move a slow task off the request thread with a guaranteed Sling Job.

## 1 — Producer

- [ ] Inject `JobManager` into a servlet or service and `addJob("academy/job/notify", props)` with a couple of properties

## 2 — Consumer

- [ ] Register a `JobConsumer` for `academy/job/notify`; log the properties and return `JobResult.OK`
- [ ] Add a failure path: return `FAILED` for a simulated transient error and watch it retry; `CANCEL` for a permanent one

## 3 — Scheduled

- [ ] Create a scheduled job (e.g. daily) with the `JobBuilder` and confirm it fires

## 4 — Queue config

- [ ] Add a `QueueConfiguration` for your topic: set it **ORDERED** with `maxRetries` and a retry delay; observe the behaviour change

<Callout type="do">Done when the producer returns instantly, the consumer processes the job (and retries transient failures the configured number of times), a scheduled variant fires on time, and your queue config controls ordering/retries.</Callout>

title: Sling Models & Adaptation
text:
A **Sling Model** is a plain Java class that maps repository content (a resource) onto typed Java fields, so your HTL stays logic-free and your business logic stays testable. ## The idea: adapt a resource to a model Sling's **adapter** pattern turns one object into another. A Sling Model is the target of an `adaptTo` call: ```java Resource resource = ...; Card card = resource.adaptTo(Card.class); // content → typed object ``` ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Resource
(JCR content node)"] -->|"adaptTo(Card.class)"| M["Card model
(typed Java)"] M -->|"getTitle()"| H["HTL reads card.title"] ``` HTL does the same declaratively with `data-sly-use`, so the component's markup just reads properties: ```html

${card.title}

``` ## Declaring a model A class becomes a model with **`@Model`**, naming what it can be adapted *from*: ```java @Model( adaptables = Resource.class, defaultInjectionStrategy = DefaultInjectionStrategy.OPTIONAL ) public class Card { @ValueMapValue private String title; public String getTitle() { return title; } } ``` - **`adaptables`** — `Resource.class`, `SlingHttpServletRequest.class`, or both. - **`defaultInjectionStrategy = OPTIONAL`** — missing properties become `null` instead of failing the adaptation. Strongly recommended. ## Why models beat scriptlets Keep logic in the model, presentation in HTL. The model is a normal Java class — you can **unit-test** it without an AEM server, and the HTL stays declarative and designer-friendly. A model returns `null` from `adaptTo` if a **required** injection can't be satisfied — that's why `OPTIONAL` + null-checks (or `@Default`) keep components resilient. We'll see required vs optional next.

title: Injecting Content into a Model
Adaptables (Request vs Resource) & Injection Style
Deploying & Using a Model in a Component
text:
A model is only useful once it's deployed in a bundle and wired into a component's HTL. ## Where models live & how they deploy Sling Models are part of your **`core`** OSGi bundle. The bundle declares the package as a model package (the `bnd`/maven-bundle config adds the `Sling-Model-Packages` header) so the Models framework scans and registers your `@Model` classes on activation. Deploy = build + install the bundle: ```bash mvn clean install -PautoInstallBundle -pl core,all ``` ## Using it from HTL `data-sly-use` adapts the **current resource** (or request) to the model and binds it to a name: ```html

${card.title}

${card.description}

``` The framework picks the right adaptable based on what the model declares — you just reference the FQCN. ## Bonus: export the model as JSON Add **`@Exporter`** and the same model serves a `.model.json` for headless / SPA use: ```java @Model(adaptables = Resource.class, defaultInjectionStrategy = DefaultInjectionStrategy.OPTIONAL) @Exporter(name = "jackson", extensions = "json") public class Card { @ValueMapValue private String title; public String getTitle() { return title; } } ``` ```text GET /content/site/page/jcr:content/card.model.json → { "title": "..." } ``` Verify a model in **`/system/console/status-adapters`** (it lists registered adapter factories) and in the **Sling Models** console. If `adaptTo` returns null, the model usually isn't registered — check the bundle is active and the model package is on the `Sling-Model-Packages` header.

title: OSGi Services
OSGi Configurations
Sling Models Best Practices
text:
A short checklist that keeps models maintainable and your components robust.

## Do

- **`defaultInjectionStrategy = OPTIONAL`** — never let a missing authored property fail the whole component; handle null/`@Default`.
- **Adapt from `Resource`** unless you truly need request data — it stays exportable and testable.
- **One model per component**, named after it; expose plain getters that HTL reads.
- **Compute in `@PostConstruct`**, not in getters — getters may be called many times per render.
- **Program to interfaces** for injected services (`@OSGiService` by interface).
- **Use specific injectors** (`@ValueMapValue`, `@ChildResource`) over the generic `@Inject`.
- **Return safe empties** — an empty `List`, not `null`, so HTL `data-sly-list` doesn't blow up.

## Avoid

- Business logic or repository writes inside a model — models are for **reading/shaping** content for the view. Writes belong in services/servlets.
- `SlingHttpServletRequest` adaptable "just in case".
- Heavy work (HTTP calls, queries) in a model that renders on every request — cache it in a service.
- Catching and swallowing exceptions silently — log with context.

## A model you can unit-test

Because a model is a plain class, a test just adapts a mock resource and asserts getters — no running
AEM. (That's the next module, *Unit Testing*.)

<Callout type="do">If a model is hard to test, it's usually doing too much. Move side effects and I/O into a service, keep the model a thin, null-safe view of the content, and your HTL stays a dumb template.</Callout>

title: Assignment: Build a Sling-Model Component
text:
Build a **"Promo Card"** component backed by a Sling Model, a service, and an OSGi config — on the
academy `core` + `ui.apps` modules.

## 1 — The model

- [ ] Create `PromoCard` `@Model(adaptables = Resource.class, defaultInjectionStrategy = OPTIONAL)`
- [ ] Inject `title`, `description` (`@ValueMapValue`), a `cta` child (`@ChildResource`), and `@Default` the title to `"Promo"`
- [ ] Derive a `hasCta` boolean in `@PostConstruct`

## 2 — A service + config

- [ ] Define a `PriceFormatter` **interface** and an `@Component` impl
- [ ] Give it an `@ObjectClassDefinition` config (currency code) bound with `@Designate`, read in `@Activate`
- [ ] Inject it into `PromoCard` with `@OSGiService` and expose a formatted price getter

## 3 — Wire it into HTL

- [ ] `data-sly-use` the model in `promocard.html`; render title, description, and the CTA only when `hasCta`
- [ ] Deploy (`mvn clean install -PautoInstallSinglePackage`) and author the component on a page

## 4 — Config per run mode

- [ ] Ship the service config as a `.cfg.json` under `config.publish/`; confirm it applies on publish only

## 5 — Export it

- [ ] Add `@Exporter(name = "jackson", extensions = "json")` and fetch `…/promocard.model.json`

<Callout type="do">Done when the component renders from authored content, the formatted price comes from your configured service, the config differs by run mode, and the `.model.json` returns your fields — all with logic in Java and a thin HTL template.</Callout>

title: Testing Fundamentals
text:
A **unit test** checks one small piece of code (a function, a module) in **isolation**, automatically.
On the front end it lets you change code with confidence — refactor, add features, fix bugs — and
get an instant signal if you broke something.

## Why bother?

- **Confidence** to refactor and ship — the tests catch regressions.
- **Faster feedback** than clicking through the UI by hand.
- **Living documentation** — a test shows how a function is meant to be used.
- **Better design** — code that's hard to test is usually too tightly coupled.

## The testing pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  E["End-to-end — few, slow, full app in a browser"]
  I["Integration — some, modules working together"]
  U["Unit — many, fast, one piece in isolation"]
  E --> I --> U
```

This track is about the **base of the pyramid**: fast, focused unit tests (with a little DOM
testing) using **Jest**.

## The AAA pattern

Almost every good test has three parts:

```js
test('adds two numbers', () => {
  // Arrange — set up inputs and state
  const a = 2, b = 3;

  // Act — run the thing under test
  const result = sum(a, b);

  // Assert — check the outcome
  expect(result).toBe(5);
});
```

## What makes a good unit test

<Callout type="do">A good test is **fast**, **isolated**, **deterministic**, and tests **behaviour/output** — not private implementation details. If a behaviour-preserving refactor breaks it, the test was too tightly coupled.</Callout>

title: Jest Basics
Matchers
text: A **matcher** is the assertion after `expect(...)`. Picking the right one makes failures readable. ## Values: `toBe` vs `toEqual` ```js expect(2 + 2).toBe(4); // Object.is — primitives & same reference expect({ a: 1 }).toEqual({ a: 1 }); // deep (recursive) equality — use for objects/arrays expect({ a: 1, b: undefined }).toStrictEqual({ a: 1 }); // strict: also checks undefined props/type ``` `toBe` compares **identity**, so `expect({a:1}).toBe({a:1})` **fails** — two different objects. Use `toEqual` for objects and arrays. ## Numbers & strings ```js expect(value).toBeGreaterThan(3); expect(value).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(3.5); expect(0.1 + 0.2).toBeCloseTo(0.3); // floats — don't use toBe expect('Netcentric').toMatch(/centric/); // regex / substring ``` ## Arrays & objects ```js expect(['a', 'b', 'c']).toContain('b'); expect([{ id: 1 }]).toContainEqual({ id: 1 }); // deep contain expect(list).toHaveLength(3); expect(user).toHaveProperty('address.city', 'Berlin'); expect(user).toMatchObject({ name: 'Ada' }); // subset match ``` ## Exceptions ```js expect(() => parse('')).toThrow(); // throws anything expect(() => parse('')).toThrow('empty input'); // message contains expect(() => parse('')).toThrow(ValidationError); // error type ``` Pass a **function** to `expect` when testing throws — expect(parse('')).toThrow() would run `parse` *before* expect sees it, so the error escapes the test. ## DOM matchers (jest-dom) The `@testing-library/jest-dom` package adds expressive matchers for DOM nodes (more in the DOM testing lesson): ```js expect(button).toBeInTheDocument(); expect(button).toBeDisabled(); expect(el).toHaveTextContent('Subscribe'); expect(el).toHaveClass('is-active'); expect(input).toHaveValue('hello'); ``` Choose the **most specific** matcher — `toHaveLength(3)` fails with a clearer message than `toBe(3)` on `list.length`, and `toBeInTheDocument()` reads better than `not.toBeNull()`.

title: Mocking
Async Testing
DOM Testing
Test Organization
Coverage & Quality
text: The last piece: knowing **how much** of your code is tested, and making sure those tests are **meaningful** — not just there to hit a number. ## Measuring coverage ```bash npx jest --coverage ``` Jest reports four metrics: | Metric | Means | |---|---| | **Statements** | % of statements executed | | **Branches** | % of `if`/`else`/ternary/`&&` paths taken | | **Functions** | % of functions called | | **Lines** | % of lines executed | **Branches** is the most revealing — it's easy to run a line without testing both sides of its conditions. ## Enforcing a threshold Fail the build if coverage drops below a bar (in `jest.config.js`): ```js export default { collectCoverage: true, coverageThreshold: { global: { branches: 80, functions: 80, lines: 80, statements: 80 } } }; ``` ## Coverage is a guide, not a goal 100% coverage does **not** mean bug-free. A test can *execute* a line without asserting anything meaningful about it. Coverage tells you what's **not** tested; it can't tell you whether what *is* tested is tested **well**. ```js // 100% coverage, 0% value — no assertion about behaviour test('renders', () => { render(); }); ``` ## What's worth testing Prioritise: business logic (calculations, formatting, validation), edge cases (empty, null, zero, boundary, error paths), and component behaviour (what happens on a click/input). These are where bugs live and where tests pay off. Don't chase coverage on trivial code (plain getters, config objects), don't test the framework or the language, and don't assert on implementation details that change on every refactor. Gut-check: temporarily break a line of production code. If no test fails, that line isn't *meaningfully* covered — regardless of what the coverage report says. ## You've completed the track Fundamentals → Jest basics → matchers → mocking → async → DOM → organization → coverage. You can now write fast, isolated, meaningful unit tests for front-end logic and components — and know when to reach for integration or E2E instead.
