title: Assignment: DOM Tests & Coverage
text:
Pick up the Jest project from the [mid-module checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) and round it out —
test a component in the DOM, organize the suite, and make coverage **meaningful**. Applies lessons
**06–08**. Budget **~1.5 hours**.

<Callout type="note">Extend the same project — don't restart. If you skipped the checkpoint, do [Part 1](checkpoint-assignment) first (setup, matchers, mocking, async); everything here builds on it.</Callout>

## Part 1 — DOM testing (Advanced)

Given a `Counter` component class (takes `element`, increments on click):

- [ ] In **jsdom**, render markup, instantiate the component, **dispatch a click**, and assert the DOM updated (`toHaveTextContent`)
- [ ] Test a **custom-event** interaction (dispatch on `window`, assert a class/text changed)
- [ ] Use `@testing-library/jest-dom` matchers (`toHaveTextContent`, `toHaveAttribute`, `toBeVisible`)

**Acceptance:** the component test exercises real user behaviour (click → DOM change); it asserts on what the user sees, not internal fields.

## Part 2 — Organization & coverage (Advanced)

- [ ] Organize tests: co-locate `*.test.js`, group with nested `describe`, share setup with `beforeEach`/`afterEach`
- [ ] Run `--coverage`; reach a sensible **threshold** (e.g. 80% branches) on the logic
- [ ] Find one **"vanity" test** (runs code, asserts nothing meaningful) and make it real
- [ ] Add a `coverageThreshold` to `jest.config.js` so CI fails on regressions

**Acceptance:** branch coverage is meaningful, not padded; the suite is grouped and readable; the threshold is enforced.

## Stretch goals

- Parametrise edge cases with `test.each`.
- Break a line of production code on purpose — confirm a test goes red.
- Add a custom matcher for a domain assertion you repeat.

<Callout type="do">Done when: tests read as a spec, mocks isolate the unit, async is awaited, the component test mirrors real interaction, and coverage reflects **meaningful** assertions.</Callout>

title: Assignment — Answer
text:
One correct solution — attempt the [assignment](assignment) first. Good tests assert on what the user sees, run in jsdom, and stay meaningful (red on a real bug, green after a behaviour-preserving refactor). ## Part 1 — A DOM + custom-event test ```js import '@testing-library/jest-dom'; // mock the loader so importing the component is side-effect free jest.mock('@netcentric/component-loader', () => ({ register: jest.fn() })); import Counter from './counter.js'; function mount() { document.body.innerHTML = `
0
`; const el = document.querySelector('.counter'); new Counter(el, { step: 1 }); return el; } describe('Counter', () => { it('increments the display on click', () => { const el = mount(); el.querySelector('.counter__btn').click(); expect(el.querySelector('.counter__value')).toHaveTextContent('1'); }); it('reacts to a "counter:reset" custom event', () => { const el = mount(); el.querySelector('.counter__btn').click(); window.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('counter:reset')); expect(el.querySelector('.counter__value')).toHaveTextContent('0'); }); }); ``` ## Part 2 — Organization & coverage ```js // jest.config.js module.exports = { testEnvironment: 'jsdom', collectCoverage: true, coverageThreshold: { global: { branches: 80, functions: 80, lines: 80, statements: 80 } } }; ``` ```bash npx jest --coverage # fails the run if branches/lines drop below 80% ``` Co-locate `*.test.js` next to the source, name tests as behaviour sentences, and gut-check coverage by breaking a line of production code — if no test goes red, that line isn't *meaningfully* covered.

title: Checkpoint: Logic, Mocking & Async
Checkpoint — Answer
text:
<Callout type="note">Attempt the [checkpoint](checkpoint-assignment) first. The [final assignment](assignment) adds DOM testing and coverage on top of these.</Callout>

## Pure logic & matchers

```js
import { formatPrice } from './money.js';

describe('formatPrice()', () => {
  it('formats cents as two decimals', () => {
    expect(formatPrice(4250)).toBe('42.50');
  });
  it('treats 0 as a valid amount', () => {
    expect(formatPrice(0)).toBe('0.00');
  });
  it('throws on a negative amount', () => {
    expect(() => formatPrice(-1)).toThrow('invalid amount');
  });
});
```

## Mocking fetch + async

```js
import { loadUser } from './users.js';

afterEach(() => jest.resetAllMocks());

it('loads and parses the user', async () => {
  global.fetch = jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ ok: true, json: async () => ({ id: 1, name: 'Ada' }) });
  const user = await loadUser(1);
  expect(fetch).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/api/users/1');
  expect(user).toEqual({ id: 1, name: 'Ada' });
});

it('rejects when the response is not ok', async () => {
  global.fetch = jest.fn().mockResolvedValue({ ok: false });
  await expect(loadUser(99)).rejects.toThrow('not found');
});
```

## Fake timers (debounce)

```js
import { debounce } from './debounce.js';

it('fires once after the delay', () => {
  jest.useFakeTimers();
  const fn = jest.fn();
  const debounced = debounce(fn, 300);

  debounced(); debounced(); debounced();
  expect(fn).not.toHaveBeenCalled();   // nothing yet

  jest.advanceTimersByTime(300);
  expect(fn).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(1); // exactly once

  jest.useRealTimers();
});
```

<Callout type="do">No real network, async is awaited, timers are faked — the suite is deterministic and tests behaviour, not implementation.</Callout>

title: The Critical Rendering Path
text:
The **critical rendering path** is how quickly the browser can turn your HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
into the first pixels on screen. Understanding it is the foundation of every other optimisation in
this track.

## How a page renders

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  HTML["HTML"] --> DOM["DOM"]
  CSS["CSS"] --> CSSOM["CSSOM"]
  JS["JavaScript"] -.modifies.-> DOM
  JS -.modifies.-> CSSOM
  DOM --> RT["Render tree"]
  CSSOM --> RT
  RT --> L["Layout"] --> P["Paint"] --> C["Composite"]
```

The steps the browser performs:

1. Construct the **DOM** from the HTML.
2. Construct the **CSSOM** from the CSS.
3. Apply any **JavaScript** that alters the DOM or CSSOM.
4. Build the **render tree** from the DOM and CSSOM.
5. Run **layout** (where each element fits).
6. **Paint** the pixels.
7. **Composite** overlapping layers and draw to screen.

The fewer resources the browser must wait for — and the smaller they are — the sooner the initial
render happens.

## What's on the critical path?

Before the browser can complete the **initial render**, it must download some critical resources:

- Part of the HTML.
- **Render-blocking CSS** in the `<head>`.
- **Render-blocking JavaScript** in the `<head>`.

For that first render, the browser typically does **not** wait for:

- All of the HTML.
- Fonts.
- Images.
- Non-render-blocking JavaScript (e.g. `<script>` at the end of the body, or `defer`/`async`).
- Non-render-blocking CSS (e.g. a `<link>` with a `media` value that doesn't match the viewport).

<Callout type="note">Goal: keep the critical path short. Minimise the *number*, *size*, and *blocking-ness* of the resources needed for that first paint.</Callout>

## Render-blocking resources

Some resources are so critical that the browser **pauses rendering** until it has processed them.
**CSS is render-blocking by default**: when the browser encounters CSS — inline in a `<style>`, or
an external `<link rel="stylesheet">` — it won't render more content until that CSS is downloaded
and processed.

You can make a stylesheet **non-render-blocking** by giving its `<link>` a `media` attribute whose
value doesn't match the current conditions:

```html
<!-- Only render-blocking when printing; not for the initial screen render -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="print.css" media="print">

<!-- Only blocks on wide viewports -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="wide.css" media="(min-width: 60em)">
```

<Callout type="do">Ship only the CSS the page actually needs to render, keep render-blocking JavaScript out of the `<head>`, and split off non-critical styles with `media`. Everything in this track builds on shortening this path.</Callout>

<Callout type="note">This track is adapted from Google's [web.dev — Learn Performance](https://web.dev/learn/performance) and the Netcentric Web Performance course.</Callout>

title: Optimize Resource Loading
text:
Once you know what's on the critical path, the next job is to make those resources **smaller,
fewer, and more discoverable**.

## The preload scanner

As a page loads, the browser runs a secondary parser — the **preload scanner** — that scans the raw
HTML and speculatively fetches resources *before* the main parser reaches them (e.g. it can start
downloading an image or script while the parser is blocked on CSS).

To benefit, **critical resources must be in the HTML markup the server sends**. The preload scanner
**cannot** discover:

- Images set via CSS `background-image`.
- Scripts/modules injected by JavaScript or `import()`.
- Markup rendered on the client by JavaScript.
- Resources pulled in by CSS `@import`.

## Minification

**Minifying** removes spaces, comments, and other invisible characters to shrink files so they
download faster.

- **CSS** minification strips whitespace/comments.
- **JavaScript** minification goes further — it also shortens symbol names ("uglification").

<Callout type="do">Minification is a build-time concern. In this project the front-end build (and AEM clientlibs) minify CSS/JS for you — make sure it's enabled for production.</Callout>

## Remove unused CSS

Before rendering, the browser must download **and parse all stylesheets** — including rules unused
on the current page. If a bundler combines everything into one file, users download far more CSS
than they need.

Removing unused CSS helps twice: less to **download**, and a smaller **render tree** to build.

<Callout type="note">Use Chrome DevTools' **Coverage** tool (and Lighthouse "unused CSS") to see how much of your CSS a page actually uses.</Callout>

## Avoid CSS `@import`

```css
/* Don't do this: */
@import url('style.css');
```

An `@import` lets one stylesheet pull in another — but the imported file is only discovered **after**
the first stylesheet downloads and parses. That makes it a **late-discovered, render-blocking**
resource the preload scanner can't see.

<Callout type="do">Use a `<link rel="stylesheet">` in the HTML instead — it's part of the HTML response, so it's discovered far sooner.</Callout>

## Inline critical CSS

**Critical CSS** is the styling needed to render the content in the initial viewport. Inlining it in
a `<style>` in the `<head>` removes a network request and can speed up first load (especially on a
cold cache); the rest of the CSS is loaded asynchronously or appended at the end of the body.

The trade-offs are real:

- Which styles are "critical", and for which viewport(s)? Can it be automated?
- Inlined CSS adds bytes to every HTML response and **isn't cached** across pages the way an
  external stylesheet is.

<Callout type="warn">Critical CSS can be hard to extract and maintain. Measure before and after — the win has to be worth the complexity.</Callout>

## Render-blocking JavaScript

A `<script>` without `defer` or `async` **blocks parsing and rendering** until it downloads, parses,
and executes. Inline scripts block the parser too.

```html
<script src="app.js" defer></script>   <!-- runs after HTML is parsed, in order -->
<script src="analytics.js" async></script>  <!-- runs as soon as it's downloaded -->
```

- **`async`** — parsed/executed immediately once downloaded (order not guaranteed).
- **`defer`** — executed only after HTML parsing finishes, in document order.
- `<script type="module">` is deferred automatically.

<Callout type="do">Keep blocking scripts out of the `<head>`. Prefer `defer` for app scripts that depend on the DOM; use `async` for independent, order-agnostic scripts like analytics.</Callout>

title: Resource Hints
text: Resource hints let you **assist the browser**: connect early, fetch critical things sooner, or fetch likely-next things ahead of time. ## `preconnect` Opens a connection to **another origin** you'll fetch critical resources from (a CDN, a font host) **as early as possible**, before the parser or preload scanner would otherwise do it. ```html ``` If a page has many cross-origin resources, `preconnect` only the **most critical** origins — each open connection has a cost. ## `preload` Initiates an **early, high-priority** request for a resource needed to render the current page: ```html ``` Use it for **late-discovered critical resources** — font files, CSS pulled via `@import`, or a `background-image` that's the LCP element — things the preload scanner can't find because they're referenced inside other resources. Don't over-preload. Too many `preload`s steal bandwidth from other critical resources, and you may fetch things the page doesn't need. ## `prefetch` Initiates a **low-priority** request for a resource likely needed on a **future navigation**: ```html ``` Same shape as `preload`, but `rel="prefetch"` — and it's **speculative**: the next navigation may or may not happen, so it runs at low priority. ## Fetch Priority API The `fetchpriority` attribute raises (or lowers) a resource's priority. It works on ``, ``, and `

title: Image Performance
text: Images are usually the **heaviest and most common** resource on a page, so optimising them moves the needle the most. Mostly that means **sending fewer bytes** — and serving images **sized for the user's device**. ## Right-size the image (and mind DPR) An image shown in a 500×500 CSS-pixel container is optimally **500×500** — *considering no other variables*. But devices have a **device pixel ratio (DPR)**: on a DPR-2 screen, one CSS pixel is 2 device pixels, so that same container wants a **1000×1000** image; at DPR 3, **1500×1500**. Serving one large image to everyone wastes bytes on small/low-DPR devices; serving one small image looks blurry on high-DPR screens. The fix is letting the browser choose — `srcset`. ## `srcset` — by pixel density Offer several sources and let the browser pick by DPR: ```html ``` Use this when the image is displayed at the **same CSS size** on every viewport. ## `sizes` — by layout width When the displayed size changes with the layout, combine **width descriptors** (`w`) in `srcset` with a `sizes` attribute that describes the rendered width: ```html ``` - `1000w` declares the image's **intrinsic width** (1000px). - `sizes` says: above 768px viewport, the image renders at **500px**; otherwise at **100vw** (full width). The browser combines this with DPR to pick the best candidate. ## The `` element `` gives you the most control — multiple candidates with **art direction** (different crops) or **format fallbacks** (modern formats first): ```html ``` - A `` **requires** a nested `` — it carries `alt`, `width`, `height`, and is the fallback when no `` matches. - `` supports `media`, `srcset`, and `sizes`. A `media` query here is a **command** the browser must follow, whereas `srcset` is a **hint**. Always set `width`/`height` (or `aspect-ratio`) so the browser reserves space and avoids layout shift (CLS). For a deep dive on formats and workflows, see web.dev's [Learn Images](https://web.dev/learn/images).

title: Optimize Web Fonts
text: Web fonts affect performance at **load time** (large files delay First Contentful Paint) and **render time** (the wrong `font-display` causes layout shifts that hurt CLS). ## How fonts are discovered A font is defined with `@font-face`. To save bandwidth, the browser **only downloads it once it knows the layout needs it** — i.e. after it has parsed the CSS *and* found an element using that family. ```css @font-face { font-family: "Open Sans"; src: url(/fonts/open-sans.woff2) format("woff2"); font-display: swap; } ``` font-display: swap shows fallback text immediately and swaps in the web font when ready — avoiding invisible text (FOIT) and reducing layout-shift impact. ## `preload` fonts If `@font-face` lives in an **external** stylesheet, the browser can only start downloading the font *after* that stylesheet arrives — making fonts **late-discovered**. Preload to start the request early: ```html ``` Fonts are **CORS** resources — you must include `crossorigin` when preloading, even when self-hosting. And preload sparingly: over-preloading fonts diverts bandwidth and may fetch fonts the page doesn't use. ## Inline `@font-face` Inlining the render-blocking CSS — including `@font-face` — in a ` ``` Advantage over `preload`: the browser only downloads the fonts **actually needed** for the current page — no risk of fetching unused fonts. (Note: the font still waits for all render-blocking resources to load first, so if the rest of your CSS is external, that external file still gates it.) ## Self-host your fonts Third-party font services (e.g. Google Fonts) require opening a connection to **another origin** before the font can download, delaying discovery. ```html ``` Better still, **self-host**: it's usually faster than a cross-origin fetch. If you do, make sure your site uses a CDN, **HTTP/2 or HTTP/3**, and correct **caching headers** for the font files. ## Use WOFF2 — and WOFF2 only [WOFF2](https://caniuse.com/woff2) has wide browser support and the best compression — up to **30% smaller than WOFF**. For modern browsers it's typically the **only format you need**. ```css src: url(/fonts/open-sans.woff2) format("woff2"); ``` Self-host WOFF2 with `font-display: swap`, preload only the one or two fonts above the fold, and set long cache headers. That covers the bulk of font performance.

title: Lazy Loading Images & Iframes
text: Images and ` ``` - **`eager`** (default) — load immediately, even off-screen. - **`lazy`** — defer until the element is within a browser-defined distance of the viewport (tuned so it's usually loaded by the time you scroll to it). With ``, put `loading="lazy"` on the nested <img> — not on `` or the ``s. The chosen candidate is applied to the ``, which is what actually loads. ## Don't lazy-load what's in the initial viewport Only add `loading="lazy"` to elements **outside** the initial viewport. It's hard to know an element's exact position before render (different viewports, aspect ratios, devices) — but some cases are clear-cut. Never lazy-load **hero images** or anything likely **above the fold** — especially **LCP candidates**. Why it hurts: a lazy image must wait for the browser to finish **layout** to know whether it's in the viewport. So a visible `loading="lazy"` image is only requested **after all CSS is downloaded, parsed, and applied** — instead of being fetched as soon as the **preload scanner** sees it in the raw markup. For an LCP image, that delay directly worsens LCP. Rule of thumb: **eager (omit `loading`) for above-the-fold and LCP images; `lazy` for everything below the fold and for non-critical iframes.**

title: Exercise: Web Performance Challenge
text:
Time to apply everything. The **Web Performance & Accessibility Challenge** is a real site,
deployed on GitHub Pages, **deliberately full of bad practices**. Your job: find and fix as many
issues as you can.

- 🔗 **Live site:** [netcentric.github.io/web-performance-challenge](https://netcentric.github.io/web-performance-challenge)
- 📦 **Repository:** [github.com/netcentric/web-performance-challenge](https://github.com/netcentric/web-performance-challenge)

## How to do it

1. **Fork / clone** the repository.
2. **Measure first** — get a baseline before changing anything. Run it through
   [Lighthouse](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/) (Chrome DevTools) and
   [WebPageTest](https://www.webpagetest.org/), and note the Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP).
3. **Find issues** — read the HTML/CSS/JS with this track's lessons in mind.
4. **Fix them** one at a time, **re-measuring** after each change so you can see the impact.
5. **Deploy** your fork to GitHub Pages (publish with a GitHub Actions workflow — see the
   [Pages docs](https://docs.github.com/en/pages)) and compare against the baseline.

<Callout type="do">Always **measure before and after**. Performance work without numbers is guesswork — the goal is a measurable improvement in the Core Web Vitals.</Callout>

## What to look for

Map each lesson to things to hunt down on the challenge site:

<Reveal title="Show the checklist">

- **Critical rendering path** — render-blocking CSS/JS in the `<head>`; scripts missing `defer`/`async`.
- **Resource loading** — unminified CSS/JS; unused CSS; CSS `@import`; opportunities to inline critical CSS.
- **Resource hints** — missing `preconnect` to third-party origins; LCP image not `preload`ed or not `fetchpriority="high"`.
- **Images** — oversized images; missing `srcset`/`sizes`/`<picture>`; missing `width`/`height` (causing CLS); legacy formats instead of WebP/AVIF.
- **Web fonts** — render-blocking font CSS; no `font-display: swap`; not preloaded; missing `crossorigin`; not WOFF2.
- **Lazy loading** — below-the-fold images/iframes not `loading="lazy"`; **hero/LCP image wrongly lazy-loaded**.

</Reveal>

## Measure with

- **Lighthouse** — quick lab audit + specific opportunities, right in DevTools.
- **WebPageTest** — detailed waterfall, filmstrip, and Core Web Vitals from real locations/devices.
- **Chrome DevTools** — Coverage (unused CSS/JS), Network (waterfall, priorities), Performance panel.

<Callout type="note">The original challenge also includes **accessibility** issues. Performance is the focus here; a dedicated Accessibility track will cover the a11y side in depth.</Callout>

<Callout type="do">Goal: a faster site with better Core Web Vitals than your baseline — and the ability to **explain *why*** each fix helped, using the concepts from lessons 01–06.</Callout>

title: Workflow Model vs Instance, & Triggering
text:
An AEM **workflow** automates a sequence of steps over a piece of content. Two words to keep straight: the **model** (the design) and the **instance** (one running execution). ## Model vs instance - **Workflow model** — the *definition*: an ordered set of steps, stored under `/var/workflow/models` (and authored in `/conf`). Like a class. - **Workflow instance** — one *execution* of a model against a **payload** (usually a page or asset path). Like an object. It has state, a current step, and a history. ```mermaid flowchart LR M["Workflow Model
(definition / steps)"] -->|instantiate with a payload| I["Workflow Instance
(running execution)"] I --> S1[Step 1] --> S2[Step 2] --> S3[Step 3] --> Done([Completed]) ``` ## What triggers an instance ```mermaid flowchart TD T1["Manual
(author starts a workflow)"] --> I[Workflow Instance] T2["Workflow Launcher
(on node created / modified)"] --> I T3["Code / API
(WorkflowSession.startWorkflow)"] --> I ``` - **Manually** — an author selects content → *Create Workflow*. - **Launcher** — a rule that auto-starts a model when a node under a path is created/modified (e.g. start *DAM Update Asset* when an asset is uploaded). Configured at `/libs/.../workflow/launcher`. - **In code** — start it programmatically: ```java WorkflowSession wfSession = resolver.adaptTo(WorkflowSession.class); WorkflowModel model = wfSession.getModel("/var/workflow/models/academy/review"); WorkflowData data = wfSession.newWorkflowData("JCR_PATH", "/content/academy/en"); wfSession.startWorkflow(model, data); ``` Use a **launcher** for "whenever X happens, run this" automation; start in **code** when a service decides to kick off processing. Reserve **manual** start for human-initiated flows like review/publish requests. Asset processing (renditions, metadata) is itself a workflow — *DAM Update Asset* — started by a launcher when you upload to the DAM. You met its output (renditions) in *Advanced AEM*.

title: Designing a Workflow
text:
You design a model visually in the **Workflow editor** (Tools → Workflow → Models), dragging steps onto the flow and configuring each. ## Step types | Step | Does | |---|---| | **Participant** | assigns a *human* task — lands in someone's Inbox until actioned | | **Process** | runs an *automatic* server-side process (Java / ECMA) | | **Dynamic Participant** | picks the assignee at runtime via a chooser | | **OR Split** | branches down one path based on a condition | | **AND Split** | runs multiple branches in parallel, re-joined later | | **Container** | runs another workflow model as a sub-flow | | **Goto** | jumps to another step (loops, retries) | ## A worked example A simple review-and-publish model: ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Payload: a page]) --> Review["Participant Step
Review content"] Review --> Decide{OR Split: approved?} Decide -->|yes| Activate["Process Step
Activate page"] Decide -->|no| Notify["Process Step
Notify author"] Activate --> End([Complete]) Notify --> End ``` ## Stages A model can declare **stages** (e.g. *Draft → In Review → Approved*) shown as a progress bar on the instance — handy for long, human-driven flows so everyone sees where the payload is. Keep models small and composable: a **Container** step lets you reuse a sub-workflow instead of copying steps. Use an **OR split** for branching decisions and an **AND split** only when branches are genuinely independent. Participant steps block until a human acts — great for approvals, but don't put one in an automated, high-volume flow or instances pile up in inboxes. For pure automation, chain **process** steps.

title: Custom Workflow Process (Java)
Workflow Best Practices
text:
Workflows are powerful but easy to misuse at scale. A few rules keep instances healthy. ## Practices - **Use transient workflows** when you don't need history. A *transient* model leaves **no instance nodes** in the repository — essential for high-volume flows (e.g. asset processing) so the repo doesn't bloat. - **Keep process steps idempotent.** Failures cause retries; running a step twice must be safe. - **Use a service user**, never admin, in custom processes — least privilege, and it survives security hardening. - **Throw `WorkflowException` on failure** so the instance fails visibly instead of silently completing. - **Read config from Process Arguments** (`MetaDataMap`) so one process class serves many steps. - **Clean up.** Schedule **workflow purging** (Adobe Granite Workflow Purge) for completed/running instances, or use transient models, so `/var/workflow/instances` stays small. ## Don't - Put **participant** (human) steps in automated high-volume flows. - Do long-running I/O inline in a step — offload to a **Sling Job** (next module) and let the workflow continue. - Forget to handle the **payload may be gone** (deleted/moved) by the time the step runs. ```mermaid flowchart LR WF["Workflow
(orchestrates steps)"] -->|offload heavy work| SJ["Sling Job
(async background)"] SJ --> Done[Completes independently] ``` Transient + idempotent + service-user + purged is the recipe for workflows that scale. If a step is slow or bursty, hand it to a **Sling Job** and keep the workflow responsive.

title: Assignment: Design & Extend a Workflow
text:
Build a small review workflow with a custom Java step.

## 1 — Design the model

- [ ] In the Workflow editor, create a model: **Participant** (review) → **OR split** (approved?) → **Process** (activate) / **Process** (notify)
- [ ] Run it manually on a test page and action the inbox task

## 2 — Custom process step

- [ ] Implement a `WorkflowProcess` with a `process.label` (e.g. stamp a `reviewedAt` date on the payload)
- [ ] Use a **service-user** resolver; read a value from **Process Arguments**
- [ ] Add it as a Process step in your model and verify it stamps the page

## 3 — Trigger automatically

- [ ] Add a **launcher** that starts the model when a page under `/content/academy` is modified

## 4 — Scale it

- [ ] Mark the model **transient** and confirm no instance nodes are left under `/var/workflow/instances`

<Callout type="do">Done when your model routes on a decision, your custom step modifies the payload as a service user, a launcher starts it automatically, and the transient model leaves no instance residue.</Callout>
